Second Class Matter.

SALT LAKE CITY,	JUNE 1, 1889.


[Column 1]

THE WOMAN'S PAPER.

	The WOMAN'S EXPONENT was issued on the
first day of June, 1872—Seventeen years ago—the
day being chosen for its coming forth in honor of
the birthday of Brigham Young who was always
its staunch friend and advocate. He expressed
himself on one occasion to the writer as wishing
it to be kept in its original form, and referred to
the Millennial Star, saying he had always been
glad that the size and form of the paper had been
kept the same, and he wished that to be an ex-
ample for the EXPONENT, or words to that effect.
We are aware that there are many of our readers
who view this matter differently, and have been
anxious to see the homely little paper in better
dress and style, and in the form and keeping of a
magazine of literature. But the EXPONENT is for
the benefit, education and development of thought
of all the sisters in the Church, and to make it
correspond to the needs of the largest number,
and adapt it to their conditions and circumstances
has ever been the object of the editor. We admit
that it was our wish to make the EXPONENT a
weekly, and keep the price the same, but that, we
found at present, to be impracticable, and there-
fore decided to reduce the price in order to in-
crease the circulation which we hope may be a
successful venture, and feel that it ought to be,
as it is the only paper in the Territory published
at a dollar a year. Also at present, the only
woman's paper.
	Since the woman's paper has first been pub-
lished it has certainly been a power and an influ-
ence for good among the people. Many are the
testimonies we have received of its doing a good
missionary work, outside in the world, and in
many a lonely cottage in the new settlements and
colonies it has carried joy and comfort with it, and
it has certainly been the means of developing much
talent among our sisters. There are those men
and women, who are now among the ablest authors
in the Territory who published their first efforts in
the EXPONENT. And there are many important
reasons why it should be better sustained. It
contains the history of the organizations of the
women of the Church, and in a great measure the
history of women themselves, and it also em-
bodies the expression of the thoughts, feelings
and convictions of the majority of the sisters.
	The Mormon women are considerably known
through the EXPONENT. It tells of them, and
speaks forcibly in the hands of the strangers.
Much truth has been inculcated through its
pages, and from strangers who have obtained
copies while visiting here, we have often received
letters of inquiry asking for further information.
Whatever awakens thoughts upon "Mormonism"
is assuredly worth cultivating. The humblest
sister has the opportunity to bear her testimony

[Column 2]

to the world in its columns; and this written
testimony cannot be in vain. It is a portion of
good seed sown, and perchance some of it may
fall in rich soil, and spring up and bear fruit to
the honor and glory of God.
	The cultivation of literature in every age of the
world and among every people is one of the chief
sources of refinement and advancement. Time
was when it was not considered proper for women
to edit papers or write for them; but that day has
past, and at the present time there are quite a
number of papers, and solely devoted to Woman's
Work and Advancement. And every person who
desires to see the women of Zion make rapid pro-
gress in knowledge and intelligence, should re-
member that much of this is done through the
press. There is far too little attention given to
home reading and home conversation upon topics
of interest. If much more time was devoted to
educational culture, and much less to frivolous
games and nonsensical talk, the wisdom of this
generation would be increased, and more lofty
and sublime ideas find expression, in language
that would be exalting and purifying in its tones.
	With the new volume of the EXPONENT, let us
hope for an increase of interest in woman's de-
partment of literature, and the sisters' aid in sus-
taining that, which is for their own special benefit
and advantage, and to give them scope and op-
portunity to express views, opinions, and truths,
on all the variety of subjects that are suited to
their tastes, inclinations and circumstances.
	We trust the Presidents of the Relief Society
in all the several branches, will use their influence
to extend the circulation, and help the agents to
perform their duties in this respect, and if they
do, it will be an easy matter for all concerned.
Many hands make light work.


PRIMARY CHILDRENS' DAY.

	The first day of June is a sort of red letter day
among the Latter-day Saints, on account of its
having been the day when Brigham Young was
born. In his life time his family were in the habit
of celebrating it in some way in which all could
participate, and since his death some recognition
of it has been made by them, when it was at all
practicable, to bring the family together.
	For a few years past the Primary Associations
have made some sort of demonstration on that day
throughout the Stakes of Zion, and it has become
so general as to be spoken of as the Primary
Childrens' Day. It is a very proper thing for
them to make this a general holiday even in pre-
ference to May Day as in this climate the first of
May is almost too cold to admit of climbing the
hills for wifd-flowers or having any out-door exer-
cises. The firsts of June is usually warm and
bright and the flowers are in the height of bloom
and perfection of sweetness.
	This year the children in Salt Lake Stake have
been disappointed in their day on account of the
Young Men's having appointed their annual
conference to commence then and engaging the
services of two or three hundred of the children
under Brother Stephens training to sing for them.
However they will not be deprived of the pleasure
of a day in Liberty Park as it has been arranged
to gather the children of Salt Lake Stake there
on the following Saturday, June 8th, a band of
music will be engaged to play, and there will be
exercises and games for their amusement, and it
will no doubt be a gala day to the little ones.
President Ellen C. Clawson and her Counselors
and a select committee will have general supervi-
sion and will spare no pains to make it.a complete
success. The different Primary Associations must
provide suitable persons to take special charge of
the children so that there will be no danger from
carelessness or accidents, and the President and
her committee will be relieved of any anxiety in
reference to these matters.


EDITORIAL NOTES.

	We have also received an exquisite bouquet of
mountain wild flowers that gladdened the eye and
brought up many tender memories. These were
the gift of Miss Annie Christiansen of Farming-
ton.
	We acknowledge with much pleasure the gift of
a box of choice flowers and among them a perfect
fig on its stem, from Mrs. M. O. Kelting of
Southern California. The perfume was so intense
that its sweetness filled the house. Many thanks
for the delicate favor.
	On Tuesday 7th, of May in company with
President Zina D. H. Young of this City, and
Sisters Sarah S. Richards and Hattie C. Brown
of Ogden we visited Hooper, Weber County, to
attend a meeting of the Relief Society, Mrs.
Adeline Belnap presiding. There was a good at-
tendance of the sisters in the vicinity though the
day was disagreeable which very likely hindered
many from coming, We dined at Bishop Belnap's
and enjoyed the visit there very much on account
of meeting there one of the illustrious women of
the early days in the Church, Sister Mary Knight
widow of Vincent Knight mentioned in the Book
of Doctrine and Covenants. We also had an in-
teresting conversation about Mexico with Brother
Stowell who drove the carriage over from Ogden
with Sisters Richards and Brown and took us to
and from Hooper Switch to our destination.
	On the 24th of May Bishop Millen Atwood of
the 13th Ward, attained the age of seventy-two
years. And in his honor, the young people of
the Ward under the care and management of his
Counselor, Nelson Empey, prepared a party for
him; and all was arranged so quietly, that it
proved a perfect surprise, and the affair was in-
deed a complete success, and very enjoyable.
The tables were handsomely decorated with 
flowers, and the refreshments were dainty and
tempting. President Wilford Woodruff and Pre-
sident Geo. Q. Cannon were present, and each
made a few congratulatory remarks. Apostle F.
D. Richards pronounced the blessing upon the
food, which was gracefully served by the young
ladies of the ward. There had been a programme
prepared for the evening including an address by
Dr. Barney, which we will give in our next issue.
Meantime, the EXPONENT extends hearty  con-
grattulations to the venerable Bishop, with many
happy birthday anniversaries in the future.
	We have examined the work entitled "Treasure
House of Useful Knowledge," compiled and
edited by Henry B. Scrammell, assisted by experts
in every deparment. It most assuredly is mul-
turn in parvo, and would be a most useful book in
the household. It is divided into six general
departments, which include specific, brief instruc-
tion in a sort of practical way. First, Chemistry
and Health; Second, Domestic Life; Third, Farm
Interests; Fourth, Mechanical Arts; Fifth, Mer-
cantile Life; Sixth, Refinements. Under these
several headings will be found much valuable in-
formation, given in a way that will perhaps save
one desiring to look up the matter, going over
half a dozen volumes. In these days, when time
seems so important a factor, a book of this kind
ought to sell well. Prices. $3.50; plain cloth
binding; $4.00; gilt cloth binding; $4.75, half 
morroco, library. The book contains 1,616 pages,
and is in excellent plain type for reading. H. A.
Hess has the general agency for the Territory.
	On Friday, April 26th, 1889, at 4.p.m. the an-
nual meeting of the Primary Association of the
13th Ward was held in the Assembly Rooms.
Miss Kate Wells presiding. Singing, "The
Childrens Song Prayer," and Bishop Atwood
offered the opening prayer; children sang "Song
of the Seasons." Minutes read, and accepted.


Programme, "Address to the Primary" Johnnie
Merrill. Song, Irene Merrill. Questions on the
first Primary answered by Ray Grant; Essay on
"Truth," Charlie Peterson.  Song, Agnes Kenner,
"Rockaby Baby," accompaniment by Miss Strom-
berg. Questions asked by Miss Agnes McDonald
answered by the children. Recitation Lettie
Carter, "Let the old cat die." Song, Lilie and
May Burt. Dialogue "The Mormon Missionary,"
Misses Aggie McDonald, May Merrill, Charlie
A. Wells, Gertie Peterson C. Reading and Victor
Stromberg. Remarks were made by Sisters
Lilie Freeze, Ellen C. Clawson, Zina D. H.
Young. The children sang, "We are the children
of the Saints," then brief addresses from Sisters
B. W. Smith, E. B. Wells and Relief C. Atwood.
Sang, "Let us all speak kind words to each other,"
dismissed with prayer by the Bishop. There has
been a very decided improvement in this Associa-
tion during the last few years, and the President
and her aids are deserving of great credit for their
zeal and earnestness in training the little ones
in the paths of duty and obedience and correct
principles. A noble and grand labor for the young
women of Zion.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

KEEP US FROM FALLING.

	Our Savior says "Whatever is highly esteemed
among men is an abomination in the sight of
God." And to "Seek first for the kingdom of
heaven and all other things shall be added there-
to. For what shall it profit a man if he shall gain
the whole world, and lose his own soul? Or what
shall a man give in exchange for his soul?"
	Surrounded, and mixed up with the world aud
its temptations which are calculated to draw the
mind, and affections away from that path which
the Lord has marked out, into forbidden ones that
lead down to eternal misery, and to endure the
scoffs and derisions of the proud, be hated of
all men, persecuted, imprisoned, and slain for
seeking to live the religion of Christ, requires a
strength and determination unknown among the
modern professors of Christianity. The Latter-
day Saints have learned that to be saved requires
something more than merely to believe on Jesus,
that faith without works is dead, and also that it
is a life-time work to become fit for the kingdom
of heaven. Though the cost may be taken into
consideration by the converts, previous to going
down into the waters of baptism, it is impossible
for them to realize the extent of the trials, and sac-
rifices which the Lord will require at their hands
before they can become pure and spotless in His
sight, to be fitted and prepared to enter into His
rest. More particularly is this the case when they
are filled with the first love of the gospel. For
the Holy spirit makes one feel that no trial or
temptation can be too great for them to meet and
overcome. But in the day of adversity, when
that spirit is withdrawn, when they are over-
whelmed in the darkest clouds of temptation, and
heavy waves of sorrow and tribulation roar and
dash with hopeless fury upon the frail and help-
less beings, mistrusting themselves, and fearing,
and trembling lest they be swept away, then they
begin to realize more fully what it costs to be a
saint.
	Those who are firm in the faith know that God
who created every living thing, and planned the
redemption and restoration of every soul, can
willingly trust Him, knowing that He will place
no more upon them than He will give them grace
to bear, and that He tries them only according to
their capacity to endure. He has commissioned
angels who will intercede for His children and
ever stand beside them in the hour of danger, and
affliction, unless their acts are such as to grieve
and drive them away. He has given to all their
free agency, and it is every one's privilege to act
upon that agency. We all have an individual
work to perform, and it is to our own interest

[Column 2]

that we work to this end, and bear in mind, this
fact that our Maker has ordained, and made us
subject to the weaknesses of the flesh to keep us
humble. And that it is not for one to boast above
another, or say "I am more righteous than thou."
For though we may be comparatively free from
certain faults that we see in a brother or sister, it
is not improbable that we are given to just as
great, and may be greater ones. And this should
cause us to examine, and dig deep into our own
hearts for those evils, which perchance are hidden
from our sight while they are being freely dis-
cussed by our neighbors. Remembering that
though we do right in all other things and lack
charity we will be rejected by our Father in the
day of His coming, We may well ask how much
longer are we to be chastened to bring us to that
oneness which is required,and must exist through-
out the length and breadth of the church of God
in fulfilment of the great and marvelous plan of
salvation, and redemption, which we are looking
for in this the last dispensation of the fulness of
times. These are things which should absorb the
thoughts, and stir up the minds of all professing
the name of Latter-day Saint, especially by those
who have been privileged to set their feet upon this
soil, to make homes among these mountain fast-
nesses, which the Lord preserved for His people
as a safe gathering place for those who wish to
build up Zion. Those who have not yet reached
the true standard themselves should be careful in
condemning another, and realize that we are not
all constituted alike, having been born and be-
gotten under circumstances as various as are our
features, or as one star in the heavens above us
varies from the other, and upon whom none but
the Great Ruler of the universe is able to pass a
correct and just judgment. Few are capable of
being merciful as He is merciful toward the weak
and erring who, because they have wandered, or
perhaps fallen into some snare set for their feet,
are by some deemed to be past all redemption.
Many who deem themselves saints would treat
them with disdain, and even call in question those
who would act the part of the true Samaritan.
Like the Pharisees who questioned the propriety
of Christ's sitting at meat with publicans and
sinners when He answered "Them that be whole
need no physician, but they that are sick."
	This classification, which we see growing in the
midst of the Latter-day Saints, is among the
stumbling blocks in the way of the weak, whose
faith has not been sufficiently established in the
truth to cling to it, and to the behests of the Al-
mighty regardless of the acts committed by mem-
bers of His church; and this is another reason
for increased diligence on their part in seeking
for His Spirit, and that perfect knowledge that
will enable them to discern the difference between
the works of God, and those of man. The Saints
are to be tried in all things, in prosperity as well
as adveristy, and those whose carnal desires sur-
pass their love of the true riches may be gratified,
but to their condemnation. "Where much is
given much is required." I have known many
who once rejoiced in this Gospel, but as soon as
the sun of prosperity began to shine upon them,
they forgot the Giver and were lifted up in pride,
and became slothful servants, and apostacy was
generally the result. "The earth is the Lord's
and the fulness thereof," and He has promised
to all those who will seek first the kingdom of
heaven, that all other things shall be added thereto.
He, in kindness, has witheld from His people
what in their youth and inexperience would
doubtless have proven the destruction of the
majority, as was shown in the start, at Kirtland,
Ohio. "No man can serve two masters, for he
will either hate the one and love the other, or else
he will hold to the one and despise the other; ye
cannot serve God and mammon." Where His
bounties have been used properly, to the building
up of His kingdom, feeding and clothing, and

[Column 3]

making the poor to rejoice, etc., instead of seek-
ing self-aggrandisement, hoarding or squandering
it on unworthy objects, to the gratification of un-
holy lusts, His blessings upon such have been
richly poured out, temporally and spiritually. I
have known a few of this class, and I have known
not a few who might have gathered around them
an abundance of the good things of this earth,
but desired rather to spend their lives and their
all, laboring in the Lord's vineyard bringing souls
into His kingdom.
	The wealth of this world cannot bring happiness
as it has proven to thousands. Though Dame
Fortune is often prodigal. she is most fickle, and
as we read in the good book, "it is an easy thing,
in the spirit of the Lord, on a sudden to make a
poor man rich. Wealth and all things else are
from God. Many kings have sat down upon the
ground, and one that was never thought of hath
worn a crown." Nobility and excellence are to be
admired, and should be honored wherever
found; and equally odious is the class whose
loftiest ambition is to "shine in society," and
conceitedly rank themselves peers, because their
spoke in the wheel of time happens to be upwards,
and feel themselves superior in ratio. And the
mind that has become so warped and contracted,
as to measure a person's worth by his worldly
advantages and possessions, and feel it condecency
or bemeaning to associate with a brother or sister
because of their lowly circumstances here, they
will hardly be prepared to dwell with the Redeemer
of the world, who was of the poorest and the most
despised and hated,during his earthly pilgrimage,
and said to his disciples, "Blessed are the poor in
spirit for theirs is the kingdom of heaven."
"Blessed are the meek for they shall inherit the
earth." I have met many in the lowliest walks,
in my associations through life, whose brightness
and superior intelligence demanded my greatest
respect and admiration. They were often found
in possession of superior knowledge their educa-
tion being far in advance of those whose groveling
minds soar no higher than this world with its
fleeting and innumerable shams, and mockeries,
caring only for its flattery and their present ag-
grandizement.
	There will be found at the close of this short
career, some wonderful and unlooked for dis-
closures. Many a one who has appeared in the
humblest garbs and been looked down upon as
unworthy the notice of the illustrious ones who
judge of one's qualifications by their outward
adornments, will be found in positions before the
throne of God that will cause a sensation similar
to that of the Jews when they learn that Jesus,
whom they held in scorn and derision and cruci-
fied was truly the Christ.
	The following extracts from a "Revelation
given through Joseph the Seer at Kirtland Ohio,
June, 1831" are profitable for all of us "Harken
O, ye my people who profess my name, saith the
Lord your God, for behold, mine anger is kindled
against the rebellious, and they shall know mine
arm and mine indignation, in the day of visitation
and of wrath upon the nations.
	"And he that will not take up his cross and fol-
low me, and keep my commandments, the same
shall not be saved.
	"Behold thus saith the Lord unto my people
you have many things to do and to repent of for
behold, your sins have come up unto me, and are
not pardoned because you seek to counsel in your
own ways.
	"And your hearts are not satisfied, and ye obey
not the truth, but have pleasure in unrighteous-
ness.
	"Wo unto you rich men, that will not give your
substance to the poor, for your riches will canker
your souls; and this shall be your lamentation
in the day of visitation, and of judgment, and of
indignation,—the harvest is past, the summer is
ended, and my soul is not saved!


"Wo unto you poor men, whose hearts are not
broken, whose spirits are not contrite, and whose
bellies are not satisfied, and whose hands are not
stayed from laying hold upon other men's goods,
whose eyes are full of greediness, who will not
labor with your own hands!
	"But blessed are the poor who are pure in heart
whose hearts are broken, and whose spirits are
contrite, for they shall see the kingdom of God,
coming in power and great glory unto their deliver-
ance, for the fatness of the earth shall be theirs.
For behold, the Lord shall come, and His re-
compense shall be with Him, and He shall re-
ward every man, and the poor shall rejoice.
	And their generations shall inherit the earth
from generation to generation, forever and ever.
And now I make an end of speaking unto you.
Even so, Amen.
		HELEN M. WHITNEY.


[-]
WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


WASATCH AND UTAH.

	On Wednesday morning, May 15, President
Zina D. H. Young, and Sister Lucy B. Young,
left this city by the morning train, to attend the
conference of the Relief Society of Wasatch
Stake at Heber City. They went by way of
Pleasant Grove and across to Charleston where
they arrived the same evening. Next morning,
Thursday, 16, drove to Heber City, accompanied
by the Bishop, and others from Charleston, in-
cluding Sister Emma Brown, who presided at the
Conference. President Hatch, Bishops and other
brethren were present; and much to their surprise
and pleasure, Mrs. Willmirth East a dear and old
friend and acquaintance, who is now President of
the Relief Society of St. Joseph Stake of Arizona.
There was a good feeling and spirit, and sisters
Zina and Lucy Young enjoyed themselves very
much, and felt that they were warmly welcomed
by the people.
	The following day the Primary Association had
their Stake conference in the forenoon, and the
Young Ladies' in the afternoon. Saturday, the 18,
the sisters went to Wallsburg and met with the
Relief Society; then back to Charleston and held
another meeting, returning across the valley to
Provo, where Sister Lucy B. remained a day or
two.
	Monday morning Sister Mary John, President
of the Relief Society of Utah Stake, with her
counselor Sister Manila M. Daniels, accompanied
by President Zina D. H. Young, started for
Pleasant Grove early, and held meeting with the
Relief Society at 10 a.m., then went on to Ameri-
can Fork and met with the Relief Society in the
afternoon, and under the auspices of the Young
People in the evening. In the morning, May, 21,
Bishop George Halladay and a company of
American Fork folks, four carriage loads, drove
to Alpine, where a meeting was held with the
Relief Society, and back to Lehi, the same even-
ing, meeting in the Relief Society Hall. The
next morning, Wednesday, May 22, the party
went over to Cedar Fort and met with the Relief
Society there.
	This completed the tour through the settlements
south of Provo in the Utah Stake, and at each
place, there was every manifestation of welcome
and joy, that could have been anticipated, and the
meetings were well attended. Also in Wasatch
Stake, there was a good and kindly feeling mani-
fested, and the sisters have returned well and in
excellent spirits.


OUR CHILDREN.

	Poor, tired mother! I know how worn out
you feel when evening comes. The thought of
resting a few moments, without hearing the least
noise from the little ones, who have required your
constant attention all day long, almost prompts


you to say "yes" when two or three pleading
voices unite, in asking if they may go outside to
play, just a little while.
	Well,—Say "yes" but add, "I will go with you
my darlings, we will have a nice little walk, and
then come home and go to bed."
	"Oh! I'm too tired for that," you think. But,
think again. It will cost you less time, and
strength and anxiety, to go with them for a stroll,
than either to hunt them up and get them all in
again, if they go out without you; or to get them
quieted and off to bed, without a few moments
recreation in the fresh, open air, since their hearts
are set on it, and their natures require it.
	Go out with them; it will do you more good
than lying on the sofa, and wondering what will
occur to make "Willie" think of how late it is
growing, and that he ought to come in. Nearly
every home has its "Willie," does it not? And
"Willie" of course, is the "Wild Lamb" of the
flock. (The Latter-day Saints do not rear "Black
Sheep" in their precious human folds.)
	Have I convinced you that you had better leave
work, rest, and even the half hour's reading you
have been anticipating all day, and go with your
children and help them to enjoy themselves and
"be good?" If I have, now listen to the sweet
music of the glad, young voices, cheering and ex-
tolling your kindness and wisdom. You are more
than paid already, for any seeming sacrifice you
may have made for their sake.
	There is nothing like entering into the child-
ren's sports with them, to make them feel that they
have your sympathies, no other way of
controling them so entirely by force of love.
	I know some children who have their "Play
Evening" at home, regularly once a week.. This
supplies so many wants which would otherwise
be sadly felt. Always when occasion will per-
mit, the mother is with them during the entire
evening. If circumstances unavoidably occur, to
occupy the mother's time in some other way, the
verdict of the children is, "We haven't had a bit
of a nice time, because you were not with us."
Oh! mothers. Be with your little ones all you
can, while you may. It will pay you better than
any other business you can possibly engage in.
		LULA.


[Column 1]

W. S. A. IN JUAB COUNTY.﻿

	In answer to a call made by a few of the
most influential women of Juab County, for a
meeting to be held, a large number of citizens
both ladies and gentlemen, assembled at the
tabernacle, Nephi, at 2 p.m., March 11, 1889,
to organize a Suffrage Association. Miss L.
A. Schofield nominated Mrs. M. Pitchforth to
act as chairman. Seconded by several ladies,
after which the motion was put and carried.
On motion of Mrs. A. L. Bigler, Mrs M. E.
Teasdale was nominated Secretary of the meet-
ing; seconded and carried. Mrs. S. A. An-
drews then offered prayer. The Secretary then
read the call. Miss L. A. Schofield was then
elected President by unanimous vote. Mrs.
E. B. Bryan was elected First Vice Presidenst
Mrs. M. E. Neff Second Vice President; Mr;.
M. A. Cazier third Vice President; Miss
Louie Udall, Secretary; Mrs. Annie L. Atkin,
Recording, and Corresponding Secretary; Mrs.
L. A. Hartley, Treasurer.
	The following named ladies were elected by
unanimous vote, to act as an Executive Board:
Mrs. M. A. Grover, chairman; Mrs. M: E.
Whitmore, Mrs. Sarah Abbott, Mrs. W. H.
Jones, Miss Emma Adams, Mrs. Mercy Wright,
nd Mrs. Julia Paxman. Miss Louie Udall
read the Constitution of the National Woman
Suffrage Association; after which a motion was
made that the same be adopted. Seconded
and carried by unanimous vote. Mrs. Annie
Atkins read the By-Laws of the Salt Lake

[Column 2]

Woman Suffrage Association. Read by
sections and adopted separately.
	After the organization was effected, Mr. F.
W. Chappell addressed the assembly. He
said: "You have been called together to-day,
to organize a Suffrage Association. The sub-
ject under discussion, is one of vital importance,
to be entrusted with the Elective Franchise.
The ladies of America have more to do than
any one else, with the shaping and moulding
of the character of their children. The ladies
have always been true to any trust reposed in
them. If women voted it would have a puri-
fying effect upon Politics. Ladies have the
interest of the Country at heart, and I do not
think they would sell their votes for gold as
men do. How could a lady teach her children
Republican principles and form of government,
unless she had a practical experience? We
have more to fear from the ignorant votes of
our citizens, than from she muskets of our
enemies."
	Mr. W. H. Jones said he had listened atten-
tively to the Constitution and By-Laws. "The
acquiring of Civil and Political government,
is based on the home, and who has more to
do with home than woman. Men and women
should go hand in hand, and cause no conflict.
When a great movement is agitated it is always
opposed." Quoted Longfellow to illustrate
his ideas. "Man is not without the woman
nor woman without the man." Quoted from
the Constitution of the United States, the 13-
14 and l5 ammendments and explained their
meaning. "The women of Utah had the
Franchise at one time, and they did not abuse
it. Woman had an insight into the character
of man, and she would not vote for a man un-
less he had a good character. No mam who is
not a good husband and father, has a right to
a position of honor."
	Mr. J. W. Paxman said "there is nothing 
objectional in the platform adopted. Women
should have an interest in this movement.
They have my sympathy, and I will aid and
encourage them all in my power. It is no
greater honor to go forth to fight for our rights
than to stand up bravely in defense of them."
	Miss L. A. Schofield said: "every lady
should feel it her duty to make an effort to
obtaiu the Franchise. Many do not under-
stand the true meaning of Woman Suffrage.
Some think woman is trying to usurp man's
rights. Not so! She ouly desires to stand
side by side with him, and share those privil-
eges he values as inestimable. Let the mothers
understand Politics, and their children will
become better citizens, and more intelligent
voters. Was there ever a great cause started
that woman did not wield a mighty influence
in it? She should educate, and fit, and prepare
herself to walk side by side with man.
'Woman was not taken from man's head to
rule over him, nor from his feet to be trampled
upon by him, but from his side to be equal
with him.'"
	Mr. C. Sperry approved the movement of
the ladies, thought they had a right to the
Franchise. He was satisfied that it was benefi-
cial when they exercised it in this Territory.
	The ladies manifested their appreciation of
the sentiments expressed by the speakers, by
frequent and hearty applause.
	The meeting was adjourned to meet in the
R. S. Hall, at 4 p.m. May 21, 1889.
	At the close a number of ladies gave their
names for membership.
		M. E. TEASDALE, Secretary.


R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. & P. A. REPORTS 

		    SEVEIR STAKE. 
	The Relief Society conference of this stake 
was held in the Relief Society Hall in the 
second ward, Richfield, March 15, 1889, com-
mencing at 10 a.m.  On the platform were


President E. Bean and her counselers, Jane 
Spencer, and Theresa Thurber, and several 
other presidents of branches in the Stake.  
	Conference opened with singing, prayer and 
singing.  Opening remarks by President 
Elizabeth Bean; the seeretary read the minutes 
of the previous conference which were accepted. 
President Bean requested the presidents to re-
port; six presidents reported, each one spoke 
of the benefits of the Relief Society, and of 
storing up of grain; of their societies being in 
good condition, and of the good works being 
performed by tbe sisters.
	President Bean was pleased with the reports; 
thought this a time when our meetings should 
be well attended, and we should meet often.  
Said Sister C. A. Hunt had resigned her posi-
tion as secretary of Seveir Stake, and moved 
that she be released by vote, and also to give 
her a vote of thanks.  Sister Christina Sellers
was then sustained as secretary for the Relief 
Society of Sevier stake.  President Bean gave 
good instructions concerning the storing of 
grain; reminded the sisters of the counsel 
given by President Woodruff.  
	Sister Lucy Heppler spoke on the duties 
of secretaries of various associations, urged them 
to be diligent in their work and to make a re-
cord of all business. 
	At the close of the morning session President 
Bean desired all who were present to come in 
the afternoon.  Singing.  Benediction by 
Sister Lucy Heppler.  Adjourned till 2 p.m. 
	Afternoon session:  Conference continued.  
Singing.  Prayer was offered by Bishop Poulsen 
Singing.  Two more Presidents reported their 
wards in good condition.  
	Counselor Theresa Thurber addressed the 
meeting; she gave good instructions; was much 
pleased with the meeting of yesterday, the 
object being to organize a Woman Suffrage 
Association in Sevier County, hoped that we 
would all realize the importance thereof.  
	Bishop Poulsen was pleased to meet with 
the sisters; said that it needed great efforts on 
the part of the sisters to attend to all their 
duties.  Encouraged the sisters to attend to 
their meetings that they might be benefited
and cheered up; spoke of the charity of the 
Sisters' Societies.  Dwelt on the beauty of
honoring and obeying the principles of the
Gospel.  "All trials and persecutions are cal-
culated to make us a great people; it is neces-
sary for us to undergo all of them."  
	President Bean said that we had in a way
been left to ourselves; the day had come for us
to exert our energies and act for ourselves;
that we had enjoyed many privileges and we
had not appreciated them fully.  "Some day
these blessings might be taken away from us 
and we would miss them."  Bore her testimony 
to the truth of the Gospel.  "We should set a 
good example before our children.  O!  how
mnch rests upon the mothers in the training of 
the children that they might become a light to
the world." 
	President Seegmiller addressed the conference
gave good instructions on how to live, and love
and honor home and country.  Said that the
Constitution of the United States was formed
by men inspired by the Holy Spirit.  
	President Bean made closing remarks.  Con-
ference adjourned for three months to be held
in Richfield.  Singing.  Benediction by Presi-
dent Wm. Seegmiller.  
			CHRISTINA SELLERS, Secretary.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

WESTMINSTER ABBEY.

BY J. C. KITTREDGE.

	This Majestic pile, which the reflective mind
contemplates with awe, looms up before the eye,
beyond the imposing tower of the houses of
parliament. While gazing at it the mind

[Column 2]

glances back to the time of the birth and in-
fancy of the great church, when it was built on
the Isle of Thorns, by Sebert, king of the East
Saxons, who died in 616. It then descends to
a time several hundred years later—to Edward
the Confessor—and recalls the incident when
he was attending mass, of his seeing the Savior
as a luminous child—or thought he saw Him.
Owing to that circumstance, prompted by a
feeling of religious enthusiasm, while an exile,
he vowed that if ever he returned he would
make a pilgrimage to Rome for the purpose of
showing his religious devotion. Circumstances,
however, prevented him from doing so. To be
released from his oath Pope Leo IX told him
that he might build a church in honor of Saint
Peter. To carry his purpose into execution
the confessor concluded to erect a church upon
the site of that built by Sebert. He destroyed
the old building, and erected what was an
enormous one for that time, as it covered all the
land occupied by the present building.
	The foundation was laid in 1049, and the
building was consecrated December 28, 1065—
less than a year previous to the battle of Hast-
ings. Henry III was the second patron of the
abbey. It was rebuilt by him in the years
from 1245 to 1272. Edward I continued the
work which had been begun by his father. In
1502 the Lady chapel was pulled down by
Henry VII, and his own built in its place.
Queen Mary refounded the monastery and
Queen Elizabeth the school. Very little of the
original church now remains. This wonderful
building always contains for the traveler some-
thing of fresh interest.
	Saint Paul's cathedral, with all its beauty, is
second in attraction to Westminster abbey.
The latter is not only famous as an ancient
church, but more so on account of being the
resting-place of most of the illustrious dead of
England.
	There is an exceeding charm about the great
pile, for it actually records most of what has
been great in England—philosophers, states-
men, soldiers, poets, artists, and musicians, all
lie here. And the inspection of the tombs is
like seeing them face to face, for in this great
sanctuary we recall their great deeds.
	Westminster abbey contains, as one might
say, the sum total of what has built England.
Here one sees an inscription which tells of the
past glory of the nation—of one who has con-
tributed his mite toward the upbuilding of his
country. Here a chancellor of the exchequer,
under Elizabeth; there a soldier who defended
his land in a time of peril. It is curious to
observe the different styles of tombs, from the
plain forms at the time of William Rufus to 
the extremely ornate ones of Elizabeth's
day.

POET'S CORNER.
	People usually enter the section of the abbey
known as the "poet's corner" at the door where
the unfortunate Queen Caroline knocked in
vain for admittance to the coronation of her
husband, George IV. This section acquired its
name from the fact of Chaucer's having been
buried there, his monument having been the in-
augural one. Among the most important monu-
ments in this section, besides the one to
Chaucer, are those of Spenser, Shakespeare,
Milton, Goldsmith, Gray, Gay, Rowe, Thomp-
son and Longfellow.

AISLE OF HISTORY.
	The first monument we observe in going from
the poet's corner is that to Doctor Thomas
Parr, who quitted this life at the rather ripe
age of one hundred and fifty-two years; having
been born in 1483, and died in 1635. He lived
during the reigns of ten princes, namely,
Edwards IV and V, Richard III, Henry's VII
and VIII, Edward VI, Mary I, Elizabeth,
James, and Charles I. If this man were noted

[Column 3]

for nothing but years, that alone should ensure
his fame. He was a fit companion to old Jacob,
the Methuselah of France, who was born in the
reign of Louis XIV, and lived to see the
French revolution. Other prominent names
in the aisle of history are Davenant, Addison,
Jonson, Garrick, Handel, Macaulay, Thackeray
and Dickens. As we were in the abbey about
the time of the anniversary of the death of
Dickens, we saw flowers strewed upon the slab
which covers his dust—a tender tribute to his
kindness of heart.

CHOIR AND AISLES. 
	From the aisle of history one naturally turns
toward the aisles leading to the choir. Per-
haps to no part of the great edifice did we turn
with so much feeling of the chivalrous past as
we did toward the choir and aisles. The
solemn stillness of death pervades the place.
There, surrounded as we were by the tombs
 and effigies of ancient days, the lights of Eng-
lish history were thrown upon us. Looking
about, we were inspired with a certain degree
of awe. Aside from all individual memorials
of meritorious personages, there is a charm
particularly about this, one of the finest sec-
tions of the abbey, especially on a bright morn-
ing, the sun shining through the windows. To
the right, as you enter, extend chapel after
chapel—Saint Erasmus', Saint Paul's, Saint
John's, and others—lined with tombs. The
monuments of the time of Elizabeth are, for the
most part, the best; grand, as was the reign of
the maiden queen! There are represented effi-
gies of almoners, chancellors of the exchequer,
maids of honor, in alabaster and other materi-
als. These chapels were once places of wor-
ship. The last instance of carved animals at
the feet of recumbent effigies is on the monu-
ment of Lionel Cranfield, Earl Middlesex, 1645,
high treasurer to James I. There is a monu-
ment to Anne Neville, who married Edward,
Prince of Wales, son of Henry VI; who was
killed at Tewksbury. After the death of her
husband she hid and disguised herself as a 
kitchen maid, but was discovered by the Duke
of Gloucester, who married her. This was the
Lady Anne of Shakespeare—

	"Was woman ever in this humor wooed:
	Was ever woman in this humor won?"

CHAPEL OF SAINT EDMUND.
	There is a great fascination in going in and
out of the various chapels with their ancient
tombs and windows. The sight acts like a
spell upon the mind, carrying it into the re-
mote past and causing the actors in former
scenes to be present with us. In this chapel
sleep the children of Edward III. Bulwer
Lytton also lies buried here. But it is an in-
appropriate place for a modern.

CHAPEL OF SAINT NICHOLAS.
	Here lies Sir George Villars, father of the
first Duke of Buckingham. Catherine of
Valois, queen of Henry V, was also buried
here. She was the daughter of Charles VI of
France. After the death of the king she
married; not very satisfactorily, it is said. It
is a curious fact that Samuel Pepys kissed the
mummied lips of that queen; her body in his
time having been removed to the abbey. The
extreme vanity of the famous diarist may be
seen in this circunstance, as clearly as in any-
thing which has been recorded by him. It may
be of interest to the reader to see just what
Pepys says about it in his diary, volume 4, page
107. Here it is:
	"February 23, 1669—Up and to the office,
where all the morning, and then home, and put
a mouthful of victuals in my mouth, and by a
hackney coach followed my wife and and the
girls, who are gone by eleven o'clock, thinking
to have seen a new play at the Duke of York's
house. But I do find them staying at my


tailor’s, the play not being to-day, and there-
fore to Westminster abbey, and there did see
all the tombs very finely, having one with us
alone, there being other company this day to
see the tombs, it being Shrove Tuesday; and
here we did see, by particular favor, the body
of Queen Catherine of Valois, and had the
upper part of her body in my hands. ‘Im-
perial Caesar dead and turned to clay!’ and I
did kiss her mouth, reflecting upon it that I
did kiss a queen, and that this was my first
birthday—thirty-six years old—that I did kiss
a queen.”
			[To be continued.]


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


ANOTHER TESTIMONY.

DEAR EDITOR:	
	I thought if there was room in our good
little paper I would write a few more lines for
it. In my last letter to you I told you about
going to Salt Lake and having a tumor
removed, and I have felt so well and happy
ever since; I want my sisters to see the difference
between being healed by the power of the Lord,
and being cured with medicine. I know that I
was healed by the power of the Lord; I have
never suffered ten minutes with any kind of
pain since I left the Deseret Hospital; and I
do feel so thankful to my Heavenly Father
for it; I never enjoyed myself so well in my
life as I do at the present time. Since I
came home last November I have made six
quilts and six chair tidies, and knit thirty
pairs of stockings, and have done twenty-five
dollars worth of sewing on the machine, besides
getting breakfast nearly every morning, and
looking after all the house work, and seeing
that the girls kept it done properly. If any of
my sisters can do any more than that, please
write and tell me.
	I have never missed one of our society meet-
ings ever since I have been able to walk to
them. I have visited my district as a Teacher
ever since Christmas. I have been a teacher
here for twenty years and I have always en-
joyed myself so much here, and now I am
going to move to Snake River Valley to live;
our boys are getting big now and will soon
want land, and we have not enough land here.
It will be a trial for me to bid my good sis-
ters good-by; the other day I had a sewing-bee
and invited all I could, in order to get to see
all of their good faces once more before I left
them; we had a jolly nice time, and sewed
about thirty pounds of carpet rags; so now I
can have a new carpet to take with me to my
new home.
	I was just reading such a nice piece in the
EXPONENT, written by some good sister, in re-
gard to taking crying babies to conference; it
reminds me of the last time I heard President
Young speak in conference; there were several
babies crying and he told the mothers next
time to stay at home and take care of the
babies and let their husbands tell them about
the preaching; I have always remembered
that and kept my babies at home.
	As ever your sister in the Gospel,
					M. A. RAWSON.


PRIMARY ANNUAL.
	Report of the Annual Meeting of the 20th.
Ward Primary held April 19th, 1889. Meet-
ing opened by singing “Beautiful Zion,”prayer
by Bishop Geo. Romney. Singing “Love at
Home.” Minutes of the last Annual meeting
read and approved. Programme as follows’
Address, Albert Romney, “The Primary.’:
Recitation, Katie Sansome; Recitation, Levi
Richards, “The Death of Sister Toone.” Song,
Pearl and Cathie Culmer; Essay Walter Rom-
ney, “The Latter-day Saints.” Recitation,
Isabelle Romney; Dialogue between three small


boys. Organ Solo, Katie Romney; Recitation,
Richard Evans; Dialogue between six of the
members. Recitation, Hazel Culmer; Song,
Lennie Savage; Recitation, Willie Salmon; 
Recitation, Pearl Culmer; Essay, Katie Hall
“Patience.” Song, Mary Salmon and Alice
Clowes; Recitation, Ray Savage; Reading,
Plet Lambourne; Dialogue between four little
girls. Song, Rosie Romney; Essay, Albert
Romney, “How to Succeed.” The speakers
were Sisters Jane Miller, L. G. Richards, Lilie 
Freeze, Louie Felt, L. A. Wells, P. L. Kimball,
E. C. Clawson, M. A. Freeze, and Brothers C.
W. Stayner, W. Willes, L. W. Richards, C.
R. Savage, J F. Simmons and Bishop Geo.
Romney. Counselor Maggie Sharp thanked
the children for their good behavior. Closed
by singing “Marching Homeward,” benedic-
tion by Brother Savage. Refreshments were
then passed around.
					S. PUZEY, Secretary.


NOTICE TO MEMBERS OF D. H. A.
	The month of May is the regular time to re-
new the membership to the Deseret Hospital
Association, and it is to be hoped the brethren
and sisters will come forward and pay the
annual fee of one dollar, and in this way help
to sustain a worthy and benevolent institution.
There are about nine hundred names on the
book of memberships, though but very few of
them have paid regularly since the first year.
Therefore it was thought best by the Execu- 
tive Committee to remind them of the time for
payment. New members are also earnestly
solicited to join.
				     E. B. WELLS, Secretary.


THE WAY TO REST.
	Many women never rest. They seem not to
understand what rest, real rest, means. To
throw one’s self down with a newspaper or book
is not rest; it is only a change of occupation.
To sit down and keep the fingers flying over
some sort of fancy work, as if one were pursued
by a demon of unrest, is certainly not rest.
But to lie at full length on a hard surface;
arms extended at the sides, head back, with no
pillow, eyes closed, all cares, and worries dis-
missed—this is rest; this will smooth away
wrinkles in the face and in temper; this will
give an air of repose to the tired, anxious
nervous woman; this will take away many an
ache and straighten out the rounded shoulders
and craned-out necks.


There are three difficulties in authorship: to
write anything worth publishing, to find honest 
men to publish it, and to get sensible men to
read it.  Literature has now become a game,
in which the booksellers are the kings; the
critics, the knaves; the public, the pack; and 
the poor author, the mere table, or thing played
upon.—Colton.


OBITUARIES.
	Died, at Payson, Jan., 21st, 1889.  Louisa Haag, our
much beloved sister, who was born in Stuttgart, Germany,
November 8th, 1839.  Embraced the Gospel, in the same
place, in 1881, and five of her children soon followed her
example.  Emigrated to Utah in 1883, with two of her
children, to join three others who had preceded her; leav-
ing her affectionate husband and one son behind.  She
was religiously inclined, seeking after truth from her early
childhood.
	She would steal away from her home to wait on the sick,
when only girl of fourteen.  It seemed to be her greatest
desire to give relief to the poor, sick, and needy.  She ex-
posed herself to wait on others; as a lady physician she did
a great deal of good among the sick.  She was an affec-
tionate mother and always set an example before her
children which was worthy of their imitation.


RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
     Adopted by the sisters of the Relief Society—Payson
Ward.  To the memory of our esteemed sister Louisa
Haag.
     Whereas, It has pleased God in His infinite wisdom, to
take from our midst by the hand of Death, our worthy and
beloved sister, and,
     Whereas, We, her fellow-laborers, in the Relief Society
feel that we have lost a friend and Sister, be it therefore,
     Resolved, That we tender our Love, Esteem and Heart-
felt Sympathies to her family, and condole with them in
their bereavement, while we submit to the will of an All-
wise God, who doeth all things well;
     Resolved, That we spread these Resolutions on the
Society Record, and present a copy to the family.
						LAMECIA A. SCHARRER,
						ELIZABETH SCHRAMM,
						JULIA WRIGHT,
									Committee.

childbed fever, Martha Ellen Prowse; daughter o
William and Loduoky Prowse, and wife of William Miller
Born March 14th, 1862.  She leaves a husband and four
children the two youngest boys being twelve days old at
the time of her death.

JUNE
Thou art here with thy garland of roses;
	Thou art here once agnin with thy song;
Thou hast brought back the blue sky and sunshine
	The days so exquisite and long.

Thou hast called back the birds wtth their music,
	Arrayed the old forest anew,
Hast brought back the lingering twilight,
	Begemmed the sweet morning with dew.

Brought back a new pleasure in living,
	The richness of life, and the bliss;
And yet amid all the glad beauty,
	There is something, still something we miss.

O June, with thy garland of roses,
	We bid thee farewell without pain,
For thou bringest not back the glad hours,
	The heart thrills we long for in vain.

There's a silence we long to have broken,
	A stillness amid all the song;
A voiceless something reminding
	Of days that forever are gone.

There are chords we miss out of the music,
	There is something gone out of the hours;
A brightness we miss from the sunshine,
	A fragrance we miss from the flowers.

Farewell, sweetest month of the summer,
	Bright gem in the swift passing years;
We are glad for thy coming and going,
	Though gladness is mingled with tears.

For we know there will come a glad summer
	And a June that is fairer than this,
Where the "lost chords" will swell the glad music.
	And the "missing" will make up the hliss.
						MRS. H. F. THOMAS.
Hearth ond Hall.


[Column 1]
FROM  A FAR  OFF SISTER.

DEAR EDITOR EXPONENT:
     Once more I find myself attempting the task
of penning a few of my random thoughts.  It 
is now the loveliest spring weather, and the
gay little birds are daily seranading us with
their sweet melodies; but soothing as music is
I am weary of too great a variety of it tonight,
yet, at last the little ones are asleep, and baby
has forgotten her tiny teeth; yes, and the notes
of the violin have ceased in the adjoining room,
so I suppose, for at least a few moments. I may
write without interruption.
     Now if this pen would only go to work and

[Column 2]
get all these varied thoughts into a creditable
article, I might remember you much oftener;
and when I can make up my mind even to jot 
down a few small lines, I feel that while re-
membering my dear sisters, that they too re-
member me; and my spiritual strength is re-
newed; my duties pointed out more plainly.
So if one seems to write for others, it is really
self and one's own weaknesses that suggest 
much that is given.
     If there is a sister in Zion who does not think
and that too, strongly, now-a-days, she must
be lacking in sensibility.  How many pure
and elevating thoughts rise in the mind, while
the wild and unprofitable are also forced in by
the wily tempter; and how often I ask, how
much am I learning to control these same
thoughts?  For I realize I must do so ere I hope
to successfully control my every word and
make it echo the sentiments of a true and
honest heart.  How often we can see even
those we love the best, in sore distress by un-
kind words;  and although the heart may not
be as cruel as this traitor tongue would lead
one to suppose, yet, certain it seems to me, that
if every evil and unholy thought is cast out,
our words and conversation must soon be that
of a Saint; and such that  the unseen angels
might be ready to listen  to, instead of being
driven from our presence.
     Our lives are ours to make or to mar.  We
have the right to earn a salvation in the
Kingdom of God, or be doomed to eternal
sorrow in the great hereafter, as we choose for
ourselves.  Shall we throw to the winds all
prospect of heavenly joy, for  the sake of
selfish whims being gratified during our earthly
life?  No! a thousand times no.  We are only
here on earth to prepare for an eternal exist-
ence beyond the grave, and why do we let
selfishness and avarice blind our eyes that we 
so often lose sight of the goal we so much de-
sire to gain.  We know that the only safe-
guard is prayer; and we are sure of His pro-
tecting influence while we humbly sue for it;
but if left alone we may stumble and fall;
and it may be so low we can never rise again.
Many of us say,  how thankful we are for
every trial we have been called upon to pass
through, and especially for the privilege of
having lived in the "Celestial order of Mar-
riage."  But were we willing that each member
be considered equal with ourselves?  Were we
really converted to the fact that this sacred
principle was revealed  by God for our tem-
poral as well as spiritual salvation?
     We cannot remove trials from the paths of
our daughters, but much may be done to
soften their trials by their  being taught im-
plicit confidence in God and never doubting 
his justice and mercy.  We are satisfied Our
Father is more merciful than we as mortals
can be, yet we must also see that to obtain like
blessings,  all must be in the same state of
preparation; if not by performing the ident-
cal labor,  by placing ourselves as humble and 
willing instruments in His hands, and if there
is  any law we have  not accepted truly and
sincerely in our hearts we  have yet to do so
before we  may hope to live up to the require-
ments of the Gospel, or be near that state of
happiness and perfection we hope to find after
laying down our life-work,  for if dropped
ever so suddenly, it cannot be received in such
an unfinished state, but must be gathered up
and begun at the very point it was left.

[Column 3]
Then surely, dear sisters, we are not wasting
our time and our struggles to overcome the
weaknesses of the flesh, and  to make each
day's work as perfect as possible.  And
though we may not now understand, we may
be comforted by these  beautiful words of the
poet:
 
		"When we reach the shining strand
		Where the waiting angels stand,
		In the far off father-land,
				We shall know;
		In the happiness unending
		Of a blissful comprehending,
		   What our life-work meant below.

		In the fullness deep and wide,
		Weary souls by sorrow tried,
		Knowing, shall be satisfied
				In His rest.
		Finding in the perfect sweetness
		Of an infinite completeness
		   That God's way is always best."
							    M. L. S.


HEPHZIBAH.

I
	"Truth is stranger than fiction."

	His love was like that of the knights of
olden time, who were ever ready to break a
lance in love's defense; and often when beside
the hearth-stone of the wide, old fireplace, on a
wintry night, he would sit by her as she dallied
with her knitting, for womans' hands are scarce
ever quiet, and as he would recount over and
again the chapter of incidents upon the battle-
field, how he had suffered with his comrades
in arms, and how many had died of the wounds
received from the enemy, his blood would rise
to fever heat, as he seemed to see the dreadful
tragedies enacted over again, and starting to
his feet with clenched hands and eyes of fire, he
would vow eternal enmity to the foe;—but his
eye chancing to rest upon his fair, young wife,
and remembering the sacrifices she had made
for him, all the anger would die out of his face,
and a change such as an angel's presence might
bring where discord reigned, would be swift
as transition, and dropping low his eyes,suffused
with tears, his passionate voice changed to low
and tremulous tones, he would exclaim entreat-
ingly, "forgive me darling,—for your dear sake
I will refrain from recrimination, your father
was a brave and valiant soldier, though he
fought on the wrong side; but he was the soul
of honor; and though it wounded his pride to
see you marry one he considered inferior, one
of the rebels, he had been sent across the sea to
subdue, yet he loved his child too well to wreck
her happiness, and recognized that love con-
quers, when the sword has failed."
	This was nearly a hundred years ago now,
and both are lying in the old church yard un-
der the mossy sods, with broken tomb-stones,
overgrown with tangled rose-vines in the sleepy
town, whose church steeples can yet be seen
from the village, where their wedded life was
spent. The lady died in the full bloom of
motherhood, many years before her "lord," and
left him lonely and sorrowful, in the prime of
his life at the old manor house.
	Long years after, a change had been
wrought in all that pertained to the village and
its inhabitants. The home of the Major and
his once young and beautiful wife, had grown


[column 1]
old and gray, and well befitted its owner; 'twas
a rambling country dwelling, gambrel-roofed,
the eaves projecting wide, and the swallows had
built their nests year  after year in the same
nooks and corners, until they seemed as much
at home as the inmates of the house.  Outside
'twas quaint in design of no particular archi-
tecture, rather rudely built, but withal substan-
tial, and like its master had seen much service;
—the storms of many winters had beaten upon
its walls, and it was weather-worn; inside it
was roomy, home-like and commodious; the
large wide open fire-place bespoke warmth and
comfort, especially when great logs of wood
were piled on the heavy fire-dogs, and blazed
up fitfully, and the tall, thin, tallow candles
burned with a flickering light, and shadows
flitted hither and thither in the long,low rooms
with their sombre walls; then the house had
almost a legendary appearance, and one
susceptible of illusions, might readily have
been induced to believe the frightful stories of
ghosts and witches, that were the regular
winter pastime, on a festive evening in the
country houses.  The furniture of the place
was in harmony with its other appointments,
oaken and dark and heavy made;—tall chests
of drawers with awkward brass knobs, reached
from the floor to the ceiling, filled with the
family linen, that had once been the pride of
the fair young mistress; tables we should call
clumsy now, and chairs and sofas straight-
backed and uncomfortable, corresponded well
with the  other articles of furniture.  In the
long dining room, the principal living room
in the days of the first master and mistress,
when the children were around them, was a tall
majestic clock, inside which one might have
stood upright with perfect ease; the large brass
pendulum swung to and fro as leisurely as
when it counted the merry hours for the happy
family that dwelt within these, now almost
deserted rooms.  The great broad face looked
knowing and wise as much as to say.  I keep
the family chronology.  I have marked the
time of births, of marriages and deaths faith-
fully for four-score years.  I guard the honor
of the house, and know its history.  Many
times in years after when the long, low room
was deserted, and a neighbor would drop in,
the old clock was like a familiar friend, and its
tick, tick in the quiet of the old house seemed
to break the deep silence that rested upon it.
				11

Deserted halls where all was once so bright,
	Now so neglected, shadowy and gray;
For she, who made the home so full of light,
	Can come no more, to chase the gloom away.

	After six and twenty years of uninterrupted
domestic felicity, the master was left alone with
his five boys.  No daughter had been born to
them to bear the semblance of the beautiful
wife and mother, though the father had
ardently longed for one, to be the light and
comfort of his home.  Many were the plans
the happy couple laid, in that past which was
gone forever now,—to train and educate a little
girl, should the fates be so propitious as to
grant them this desire.  The husband thus bereft
of his treasure yearned more than ever for wo-
manly companionship.  His mother had been
dead many years, he had never known what it
was to have a sister; and now his wife the be-
loved of his heart, the companion of his youth,
and the pride of his home was gone forever.
He could not be reconciled to his loss, and he
gave himself up to sad and gloomy reveries that
made him old before his time, and totally un-
fitted him for the duties devolving upon him as
the father and only parent to his family of
boys.  The boys grew to manhood and entered
upon the busy, practical pursuits of life; and
one by one left the paternal roof to which the
father clung so tenaciously.  The orchard and

[column 2]
fields were neglected, the garden grew to weeds
and the fine old cherry trees were the sport of
the birds and the unruly urchins of the neigh-
borhood.  The beehives demanded some little
attention once or twice a year, but the master
never gave any orders to his servants so the
fine estate was left to ruin.  The old gentleman
sat in his chimney corner in her room, the one
she, whom he so loved had occupied, and drank
his tea, and ate his tasteless meals, (scarce
realizing what he did,) on the same round table
with snowy linen, where so many times in the
olden days they two had sat and chatted over
the romantic past and sketched out the golden
future.  There was the queer cupboard filled
with quaint old china, a few pieces of silver-
ware seldom ever washed, a chest of drawers
packed with linen that was never aired.
     For years the master lived on and on in the
same dull, stupid, monotonous wey until he
was nearly a hundred years old.  Once or
twice a year the eldest son and heir, long ago
tired of the factory which had fallen into a ruin,
the namesake of his father and father’s father,
Elisha, would come up from Boston, to look
after the property and see that no one carried
away the valuables; and each time he antici-
pated renting the estate to some thrifty young
farmer, who would make it pay a good interest,
as well as keep it in repair. “No! No!” was
invariably the answer. “Wait until I am
gone.” A younger son resided in the village,
but his family was large, though he like his
father had married late in life, and had not
been very successful in his enterprise, had met
with heavy losses, and the eldest son hated him
and would not allow any interference on his
part in regard to the homestead.  This was the
condition of affairs three score years ago and
more, at the time our story opens, and the vil-
lage was as described in the opening paragraph.
Four score years and more had flown since the
old Major first saw the light of day, and those
he knew in early life, one by one had gone un-
til none remained of the loved and cherished
companions of his youth, and he felt,

"Like one who treads alone, some banquet hall de-
	serted
Whose lights are fled, whose garlands dead,
And all but he departed."

	He was much alone; occasionally his grand
children who lived near the homestead would
come in and listen to his strange stories, but his
heart seemed cold and dead, and he never
kissed or caressed them in fact they worried
him; so the old woman used to say who waited
on him.  The little things wondered at his in-
difference and often asked “What ails grand-
pa?” And mamma would explain how he
mourned for her he had so tenderly loved, his
wife; and that no daughter had come into the
home to fill the vacancy; and it may be strange
to state that none of his son’s wives suited him,
he thought them all too common place.  They
could not enter into his feelings and views; and
had no patience with his whims.  He talked
much to himself and often fancied that his
darling, the light of his life, sat by him as in
days gone by.
	One wintry day when the snow was deep
upon the ground, he was sitting by the little
corner fire-place toying with his silver snuff
box and musing as though holding sweet com-
munion with her he loved, and a little child
beautiful and bright, came running in, and
roused him from his dreamy reverie, exclaim-
ing, “News! grandpa; good news!” The poor
old man half dreaming said, “No, my son, she
has not come back, she cannot come, I have
called her often, but all in vain;” “But grand-
pa,” reasoned the child, “papa sent me to tell
you to come over;—you must come, its to see
the new baby, it came at sunrise this morning,
and papa wants you to drink to its good health
and long life, for it is a girl, and you are to

[column 3]
name it.” Hearing this and beginning to un-
derstand the situation, the aged man took his
hat and cane, and off he strode with the little
one to the house of his son, a short distance
away, only a few minutes walk, and with the
impatience of a child he entered.  The son
welcomed his father warmly, and after seating
him comfortably by the fire, explained why he
had sent for him on such a cold raw day.
“Father” said he; “we have had sons and
daughters, six in number, this is the seventh,
and perchance may be the last in your life-
time, and I want you to name it, and filling
the glass with purple wine he handed it to his
venerable father, who taking it drank to
“Hephzibah,” and said “I shall not live long
and she who was born to-day shall bear the
name of her I loved so well and mourn so sin-
cerely.  The wee little creature shall be called
for her grandmother, who was “the pride of
the village.” And then he uttered, possibly
unknown to himself, a prophecy that she should
become learned and wise, and that in all the
village none should excel her in the road to
fame.  But of all this no one thought much at
the time, but years afterwards the saying came
back to their remembrance with increased sig-
nificance.  Dinner was served, and the old
Major grew talkative.  A tender chord had
been touched by repeating the name he loved
so well, for how dear the plainest names be-
come when associated with those precious to
our hearts, we all know.   The children in-
quired what grandpa named the baby sister,
and when they were told 'twas Hephzibah, they
though it “dreadful horrid,” and even cried to
think so sweet a little baby should have such a
terrible name given to it.  But grandpa was
inexorable, and tears and entreaties were of no
avail, and as if to make peace he said she
should have all the fine embroidered laces, her
grandmother had wrought with her own dainty
fingers, the chests of linen, the elegant, old
china, the silver ware and spoons, and all the
silks and ribbons so long packed away out of
sight, that no woman was permitted to handle,
much less to wear.  All this and more he pro-
mised on the day of her birth to the uncons-
cious little one.
		  [TO BE CONTINUED.]


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	The Connecticut House of Representatives
has passed the bill granting school suffrage to
women, with the right to hold school offices.
	The New England Woman Suffrage Asso-
ciation, having reached the age of twenty-one,
is no longer in the minority period, and is en-
titled to the ballot and full rights of citizen-
ship generally. The shingle of conservatism
still refuses to go over the dam, but the in-
evitable is near at hand.—Boston Globe.
	The Connecticut House of Representatives
has passed a bill providing that "no person
shall be disqualified for being a member of any
ecclesiastical society, with all the privileges
and rights of male members, or from holding
office in such society, by reason of sex;" also, a
bill making women eligible to the office of
Assistant Town Clerk and Registrar of Births,
Marriages, etc.
	"Mrs. Julia Ward Howe received many
beautiful gifts on her seventieth birthday, May
27, and the papers have devoted much space to
well-deserved tributes to her life and work.
The current number of America celebrates
Mrs. Howe's birthday with a series of contribu-
tions in prose by her children, Florence Howe
Hall, Laura E. Richards, Maud Howe Elliot
and Henry M. Howe, with a selection from the
writings of the late Julia Romana Anagnos."


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


WOMAN'S SUFFRAGE.

We're a band of valiant women,
	In a cause both true and just;
Have started on a mission,
	And in God we put our trust.
We aim not, at pomp and grandeur,
	But ours is the call for right—
And it has a beacon burning—
	To cheer us with its light.

'Tis the cry from far off countries,
	'Tis the wail of womankind
To aid in gaining knowledge,
	To strengthen and to bind;
To comfort and encourage,
	To raise the fallen heart,
	To lighten heavy burdens,
And of justice, grant a part.

Our duties here, as mothers,
	Give license for our needs;
Simply asking right of franchise,
	The power the ballot feeds.
We claim no greater liberty,
	Than intelligence will gain;
No surer wealth of purpose
	Than our minds can now maintain.

For woman's worth is growing;
	In time, 'twill force its way
Thro' darkness adn thro' prejudice,
	Men are groping in to-day.
Therefore, we'll work in earnest.
	And calmly bide our time
To enjoy woman's right of suffrage,
	In this our Utah's clime.

We'll not falter in our purpose,
	No difference what people say;
Ours is a glorious object,
	O'er which the future sheds its ray;
Tho' ridicule and ignoranee
	May scorn, and scoff at pleasure,
Undaunted we will meet them,
And deal them out full measure.

We'll stand, with fearless character,
	And lend a helping hand
To every call that's honest,
	Of whatever creed or clan.
Our motto and our watchword,
	Shall be principle and truth
For our home and for our country,
	God grant us help for both.

		J. P. M. FARNSWORTH.


[Column 1]﻿

A MISSIONARY.

How shall we express the great pleasure
	We take in now writing to friends?
O truly! No mortal can measure
	The blessings, which Our Father sends.

One year and a half since we parted
	From friends, with the shake of the hand;
'Tis just the same time since we started,
	From mountains and valleys so grand.

Though in a strange land now we wander,
	As young missionaries we roam;
We're growing the fonder, and fonder
	Of friends in our dear Mountain Home.

Duty called for a time, separation
	From those who are dear to our heart;
From Sunday School, friends and relations,
	From all duty called us to part.

The call was to go on a mission,
	To battle with error and doubt;
And overcome rank opposition
	To truth the world soon shall find out.

This labor and duty devolving
	On us to perform for the Lord—
We left our home firmly resolving,
	To trust in his promising word.

We went, ah yes, 'twas our duty
	To go and proclaim to the world,
The glory and transcendent beauty
	Of Zion's white banner unfurled.

To warn men of all ranks and stations,
	To show them the Gospel we teach,

[Column 2]

And tell of the great exaltation
	They may by obedience reach.

We tell them, "give heed to our warning;
	The Gospel accept and obey;
That you may arise in the morning,
	Of the first resurrection day."

We teach them true faith in the Father,
	Creator of all that we see;
And also in Jesus our Savior,
	Who says, "Believe also in me."

"For through Him you'll gain life eternal,
	If you but obey His command;
Partake of the blessings supernal,
	In reigning with His righteous band."

That they with an honest desire,
	Should turn from transgression and sin;
And make their aim brighter and higher,
	Determined life's battle to win.

Be born of the water and spirit,
	Receive a remission of sins;
Obey the Still Voice when you hear it,
	Instructing your spirit within.

And when you have laid the foundation, 
	Go on to perfection's bright shore.
That you may enjoy the relation
	Of Christ with His Saints evermore.

When "peace on the earth" will be sounded'
	"Good will to all men" be the cry,
Your joy will be surely unbounded,
	Singing praise to Jehovah on high.

Above, is the message we're preaching,
	'Tis Jesus, and Him crucified;
His goodness and mercy we're teaching,
	How, for us, He suffered and died.

In leaving our home for this object,
	To travel without purse or scrip,
The Elders are, oftentimes subject
	To insults, the scourge and the whip.

Vile jeerings, and vituperation,
	Mob violence, scoffing and scorn;
A flood of misrepresentation,
	A child of gross ignorance born.

They drive us from city to city,
	Yet, we must forgive and forbear;
They yet will come pleading for pity,
	In tears of remorse and dispair.

Remember, the word that is spoken,
	The servant's not greater than Lord,
If you are reviled, 'tis a token
	That you are obeying his word.

Rejoice ye in your persecution!
	This, Saints, shall your heritage be;
For, this, did they unto the Prophets,
	And thus will they do unto thee.

Don't think all is stormy and cloudy,
	That nothing but troubles appear;
We often meet those who are friendly,
	Who's kindnesses comfort and cheer.

For, oft, as a sweet fragrant flower,
	Is found by a rank noxious weed,
So some, will do all in their power
	Providing the things that we need.

Who giveth a cup of cold water,
	In no wise shall lose his reward.
Who gives us food, raiment and shelter,
	Shall surely be blessed of the Lord:

At last, we will turn our attention
	To brothers and Sunday School friends;
(But first we just thought we would mention
	Our labors, and what they attend.)

To give a complete explanation,
	Of scenes and experiences too,
I'll wait for the realization
	Of once again meeting with you.

Indulging in musing reflections,
	Familiar home duties oft pass;
And foremost of these retrospections,
	Are scenes of the Sunday School class.

When just before ten Sunday morning—
	Return of another Lord’s day—

[Column 3]

The Sabbath School children are formiug
	In classes to learn, sing and pray.

It seems even now, we hear blending
	The voices of Saints singing praise;
To Father in heaven, for sending
	The Gospel, in these latter days.

And when you are humbly addressing
	The throne of our Father to-day,
Dear friends, you must ask for a blessing
	For those who are far, far away.

We earnestly pray and petition,
	The Lord to direct and sustain
The Saints in their trying position,
	Till Christ comes in glory to reign.		S.


THE FIRST OF JUNE.

	June 1st, "Primary Day" among the children
of Utah, is one of the happiest in all the year.
It comes when nature is at her best and all the
flowers are gorgeous with their wealth of color
and weighted with their rich perfume. It falls
between the season of uncertain and change-
able spring, and that of heated and oppressive
summer. In our altitude it is in every respect
far more suitable for the annual spring festival
of the children than the conventional Mayday
whose pleasures it each year more and more
completely reserves unto itself.
	As the birthday of Brigham Young, the day
deserves all the honor which it receives. A per-
sonal remembrance of that great man is held
by many of the youth who do not yet feel
themselves too old to share in the festivities of 
his anniversary. The younger portion know
him only for the deeds and words which he has
left behind. As they grow in years and un-
derstanding they will become better able to ap-
preciate his worth, and in the record which he
made they will find, as their elders do, ever
more to commemorate and admire.
	It is said that cotemporary history is never
just; what is praiseworthy is smothered with
adulation, what is faulty is distorted by preju-
dice. During the life of the man whose natal
day the children yesterday observed, his works
seldom received a word of praise. He was
slandered, caricatured, and contemned, and
most bitterly, strange to say, where his achiev-
ments were apparent every day. If he was
credited at all with the magnificent acquire-
ments which are a part of Utah's history, those
who were bold enough to recognize them came
in themselves for a share of the abuse heaped
upon him.
	Happily the world grows wiser as the years
move on. Petty enmities fade away as the
cycles sweep around, and those who judged
heatedly yesterday are to-day blessed with
calmer vision. Fresh eyes, too, and minds un-
tainted by passion, have come to see and ap-
preciate what to us is an old theme. Under
these conditions the work of Utah's founder is
better understood. Brigham Young is not now
stigmatized as a fanatic, a greedy zealot, an
ignorant autocrat. Men of sense and breadth,
judging him by his works, speak of him as
possessed of transcendant powers. They admit
that he was mighty in character, and must have
been filled with true statesmanship. They
recognize in the Utah of to-day a never dying
testimony of his greatness; aud, however much
they may differ with his creed, they do not
withhold their tribute to his nobility of mind.
	As the record of his life shall be more widely
read and better understood, admiration of his
character will increase with all who are able
to approximately measure men by the divine
standard: "By their works ye shall know
them." Who knows but what on some future
occasion when the children following their
beautiful custom are celebrating his anniver-
sary, a thought of regret may enter the hearts
of some who lately deemed Brigham Young's
name unworthy of being borne by one of the
streets of Ogden City!	The Standard.
