Second Class Matter.

SALT LAKE CITY,	June	15, 1889.


[Column 1]

THIS AND THAT.

	The Old Folks' Excursion comes off on the 26
inst. It will be the 15th anniversary, and the
destination is Lester Park, Ogden City.
	We acknowledge with thanks and a full appreci-
ation of the delicate expressions of affection,
floral tributes from Mrs. Jane Cowan, Misses Kate
and E. Wells, and Mrs. A. W. Cannon; also a
very handsome and fragrant bouquet of wild
flowers from Miss Caroline and Master Volney
Crocheron.
	Mrs. Sarah M. Kimball, of this City, accom-
panied by her daughter, has gone East to the
State of New York to attend a centennial celebra-
tion of the town of Phelps, Ontario Co., the
place of her nativity. Mrs. Kimball was speci-
ally invited to represent her father's family, the
Grangers, at the public demonstration which is to
be made on the occasion, June 19, 1889.
	The name of the Salt Lake Stake Academy,
was changed to Latter-day Saints' College, by
formal action of the Board of Directors, on the
15th of May, 1889. We have received the Annual
Circular issued for the Fourth Academic Year,
1889-1890, and shall notice more fully at another
time. We have observed in glancing through it,
several changes, which mark the progress of this
excellent school, and feel confident of the success
of a college where religious teaching is at the
foundation of educational work.
	Clara Barton, President of the Red Cross, is
doing effective work on a large scale at the scene
of suffering in Johnstown. This remarkable
woman is a public benefactor, and deserves ever-
lasting gratitude for her efficient labors among the
sick and afflicted, the wounded and dying, not
only on the battle-field, but in times of peace,
when dire calamities of any kind have befallen a
place or people; whenever and wherever suffer-
ing humanity has called for gentle woman's minis-
trations, nothing daunted, she has gone like an
angel 'mid scenes' the most appalling. Her name
is written in deeds of valor and charity, far more
imperishable, than if in letters of gold, for they
are engraven unmistakably upon human hearts.
	The Alumni Association of the Deseret Uni-
versity, had a most delightful meeting and ball at
the University Building in this City, on Wedens-
day evening June 12. The programme was a
most excellent one, being a pleasing variety of
exercises, songs, music. speeches, toasts, etc.
The banquet prepared for two hundred was in
every way a perfect success; the tables were
artistically decorated with flowers which always
adds greatly to any scene, and to the enjoyment
of the participants.
	The University commencement exercises were

[Column 2]

held in the Salt Lake Theatre on Thursday,
June 13, and were of a most interesting character
of which we hope to give a more full account in
the near future; also the names of the graduates
and other items of the brilliant and successful
affair.
	The three young girls who graduated in the
literary course were, Misses Mary Lewis, May
Preston, and Maud Merrill of Salt Lake City.
Miss Preston, representing the graduates in the
literary course, read an essay "Poetry as a Moral
Influence," which was received with hearty and
richly merited applause and a shower of flowers,
which must have been very delightful. Miss
Bessie Dean sang a ballad "Laddie" in her most
happy manner, and was also enthusiastically
complimented.
	Friday was "Field Day" for the Deseret Uni-
versity at Lake Park, where a most enjoyable
afternoon and evening was spent by the teachers,
graduates and their friends; dancing at the
pavilion was kept up until a late hour.
	To the principal, Dr. J. R. Park must be attri-
buted much of the success of the institution, and
he will surely feel laudably proud of the honors
and gratitude which are rightfully bestowed upon
him and his fellow laborers, who have been so
many years associated in the University's educa-
tional work. Professors Toronto, Kingsbury and
Paul also come in for a goodly share in the
public debt of gratitude.
	In honor of Mrs. Elliot Sandford and her hus-
band, the late Chief Justice of this Territory,
previous to their leaving Utah, (and whose re-
moval from the judicial bench the people so much
regret) an excursion party to the lake was planned
and successfully carried out by Mrs. F. S.
Richards, and Mrs. W. W. Riter. There were
about fifty invitations given, and Mr. Riter
placed a car at the disposal of the company.
There was no particular programme, except
having a good time generally, and it was
thoroughly an enjoyable affair. The day was a
fair one, as most June days are in this climate.
The party seemed happily grouped and the
ladies in charge did all in their power to make
the hours speed merrily.
	A few indulged in baths, but most of the party
sat in the pavilion, chatting and watching the
bathers and admiring the wonderful lake, the
western view, and the mountains beyond, until
the late train having arrived, on which the Judges
were expected, a call was made to repair to the
dining room, where the guests were served to an
elaborate dinner, including all the delicacies of
the season.  Mr. and Mrs. W. W. Riter, Mrs.
F. S. Richards, (her husband having been unavoid-
ably detained), Judge Sanford and wife, Judge
Anderson and wife, also Miss Anderson and Mrs.
McCoy, were seated at the head of the table;
Delegate John T. Caine and wife, and Mrs. Buck
of Keokuk, were near them, and other ladies and
gentlemen filled the long table on either side, and
a merry company they were indeed.
	Mrs. Sandford had many gifts of flowers,
baskets and bouquets, and she looked radiantly
happy withal. It was very much regretted that
Judge Henderson aud wife, and Judge Judd and
wife, who were to have been of the party
could not attend; also that President Angus M.
Cannon who accompanied the party, was obliged
to return on an early train.
	On Saturday, June 8th, Judge and Mrs. Sanford
left Salt Lake City for the East, going by way
of the National Park. They have left many
warm friends and admirers in Utah, who will
gladly welcome them back should they ever re-
turn.
	All over the land in America and elsewhere 
abroad, the deepest sympathy for the sufferers of 
Johnstown has been felt, and in many places and
in many ways expressed; not only in touching

[Column 3]

and eloquent words, but also in material aid and
assistance coming from all parties, and all sections
of the country.
	Utah has responded heartily, as evinced by the
carloads of provisions sent and money liberally
donated by individuals and business firms. Also
by the united efforts of the citizens of Salt Lake
City, in planning, and carrying into execution the
Mammoth Concert in the large Tabernacle, the
proceeds of which it is now estimated will be over
seven thousand dollars.
	In this delightful entertainment for so noble a
purpose, the best local talent in the City, gave
their services with glad hearts and voices and
willing hands, to swell the fund for the benefit of
suffering humanity.
	Young ladies have not been idle, as we under-
stand quite a considerable sum was raised by
selling bouquets on the streets. Of course youth
beauty and flowers are in themselves attractive,
but when a noble purpose is in view, the enter-
prise was sure to be a grand success.
	In Ogden, a Benefit concert was given at the
tabernacle on short notice, but it was quite a
brilliant affair, and there as in Salt Lake, the best
local talent united heart and heand to make it a
success. Quite a sum was raised there too, to
swell the fund by the little girls selling bouquets,
in booths fitted up for the purpose on the streets.
All the flowers were donated from the gardens
round about. It was a very pretty sight to see
these sweet wee maidens surrounded with an
abundance of the most choice and lovely roses
and flowers of June, engaged in such a charitable
work at their tender age.
	We regret exceedingly our lack of space to
give more special details of what has been done.
Ogden, Provo, and other cities in Utah have also
raised money for the sufferers, by individual do-
nations. Some of the means we understand, is
to be devoted to the Seattle people, who were
sufferers on account of the great fire which
occured there recently.


THE EXPONENT'S BIRTHDAY.

SEVENTEEN YEARS OLD.

DEAR EXPONENT.
	And so you were seventeen years old the I, of
June, 1889? How very happy an idea that it is
also President Brigham Young's birthday. In
calling you into existence upon that honored day,
you are imperishably connected with his illustri-
ous history, as well as indebted to him for your
very being. Have you not cause for gratitude?
You, who even yet in your "teens" speak to all
the world for the maidens, matrons, and even the
Priesthood, in behalf of our liberty and our re-
ligion?
	What a traveler you have been. "The isles
wait for thee," the valleys listen and the moun-
tains look afar off, waiting for thy coming. Into
how many stranger hearts hast thou borne the
tidings of truth for thy people, and the sweet in-
fluence of the Gospel; thou faithful missionary
for the Father.
	In this brief career of seventeen years, how
much has been accomplished in His service only
He can count. Great and kind was the heart and
prophetic the mind of Brigham Young when he
called and named thee his literary daughter;
when he gave thee thy mission as bearer and
messenger of truth; as historian of woman's
progress among God's people. Forever may thy
banner lead though many others should rise amid
the hosts of thy people. Long years hast thou
been the solitary guest and comforter, the one
woman's paper; and may they never forget thee,
their first ove; never lay aside their first duty to
thee and thy founder.
	May they who bear his name, and they who
love to remember his counsel, unite their names


upon thy pages with their rarest thoughts, as they
would bring their gifts to deck beauty's form.
Let thy columns be the repository the archives of
treasured thoughts, and records wrought from the
minds and the hearts of the daughters of thy peo-
ple.  Soon, no longer maiden, but woman, dear
EXPONENT, may thy presence be welcomed in
ten thousand homes, telling what Zion's daughters
have done, the magnitude of their enlarging
fields of usefulness, and their still grander hopes.
When you are eighteen, dear EXPONENT, no
take time—when you are twenty, perhaps, may be
you will add a fold or two to your skirts, and em-
bellish them with journalistic attractions—for, are
you not, by birthright entitled and endowed to
wear as many honors as any other eldest born
daughter of fame?

	At this point in my paying my respects to the
EXPONENT, the other guests could not restrain
their impatience at my appropriating so much
valuable time, and I felt my skirt getting pulled
by some one, while others crowded past me with
something in their hands, and a general murmur
of pleasant voices rising higher and higher,
abashed me into silence, and while the EXPO-
NENT peacefully smiled acknowledgement of my
humble appreciation and good wishes, which was
all I had to offer, (as is generally the case on
every other note-worthy occasion,) she was
obliged to give directions to some one to summon
several persons to make out receipts, and in less
than a minute, there was such a fluttering of
papers, exchanging hands, and such a clinking of
falling coins, that I sank back in my chair, over-
come with my insignificance, when the springs
seemed to sink so entirely away, that I sprang up
in alarm. Gracious! was the EXPONENT'S birth-
day reception all a dream? Well, then, as sure
as I'm saying this to you, that dream means
something; "its the forerunner of a good sign,"
as a dear old lady I once knew used to say.  And
I believe this same dream was brought about by
my reflections on the reduction in the price of
the EXPONENT. I wouldn't have done it. A
Mormon Woman's paper is worth two dollars,
more than some other papers are worth one dollar.
		AUGUSTA JOYCE CROCHERON.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

PRIMARY AT MILL CREEK.

	On Tuesday June 4th, we had the pleasure of a
visit to the Primary Associations of Mill Creek
Ward, the four districts having come together, as
is their usual custom to hold an annual meeting.
There were present of visitors President Ellen C.
Clawson and her Counselor Mrs. C. Cobb also Dr.
R. B. Pratt. The meeting house presented an
inviting appearance with the beautiful children in
summer attire, and the vases filled with roses and
fragrant flowers from the field and garden. The
exercises were varied and a credit to the Associa-
tion. Each President had charge of the children
from her own district. The meeting opened with
singing, "All nature invites her praise to God."
Prayer by Elder Phillip Carlisle, singing, "Nearer
my Savior to Thee." Minutes read by the Secre-
tary and approved, also the yearly report. Pro-
gramme first district Mrs. Ann Lemon presiding;
Gertrude Cook, Washington Lemon and Mary
Fuller each gave a recitation, singing by all the
children "The Word of Wisdom," President
Clawson spoke a few words on this subject. Pro-
gramme second district, Mrs. McAllister presid-
ing exercises as follows: Lizzie Bowden, recita-
tion, "Annie and Willie's Prayer," Celia Jen-
son, Earnest Jenson recitation, song by several
girls. Rhoda Ann Boam recitation, Etta Jenson
essay on "Modesty." Song several little girls
"Sabbath  Day." Robie Hamilton, Arthur
Williams, Bella Jenson each a recitation, instru-
mental music by one of the girls, song "The

[Column 2]

Lingering Lover" Ethel Gertrude Park. One of the
Counselors to Mrs. Mary McAllister, Mrs. Cecelia
Sutherland nee Samuels was honorably released
on account of moving from the ward, and a vote
of thanks given her for faithful performance of
duty; Mrs. Jane Ann Park was appointed
Treasurer in the place of Mrs. Mary Samuels, who
was also honorably released from the position.
Singing and benediction closed the morning exer-
cises. Ample refreshments had been prepared in
the vestry, and the noon hour was profitably spent
in general conversation and the meeting of sisters
and friends socially.
	Afternoon session: opened by singing "Love
At Home," prayer by Elder Sorenson, President
Y. M. Association at Mendon; song, "The
World's Jubilee." Programme third district, Mrs.
Frances Hanson, and Mrs. Mary Ann Birch;
Vivian and Ginevra Hendrickson sang "Bright
Smiling Morning," George and John Home,
dialogue, little Mary Bess, song "I had a little
letter," Vilate Morgan and Maud White, dialogue,
entitled "Jennie and Carrie," Gertie Winder song
"Good Night." Mrs. Cobb presented the name
of Mrs. Anzina Peterson to fill the place made
vacant by the resignation of Mrs. Celestia Hanson
who had moved away. Mrs. Peterson was
unanimously sustained and Mrs. Hanson given
a vote of thanks by the children; Mrs. Cobb
afterwards made some very appropriate remarks
on order, obedience and the result of the same.
Bible questions and answers by Roy White and
Birdie Clyde, Lottie Fowler and Mary Fairborne
dialogue, Pearl Carlisle, song. President Hanson
stated that they had intended to have had a manu-
script paper prepared, but had not succeeded in
getting it into shape, however some of the articles
sent in for it, were read by the secretary, Miss
Belle White. Mrs. Louisa Snedekar sent in her
resignation as secretary, which was accepted and
Miss Anna Bell was sustained to fill the position
of secretary. Brief remarks suitable to the occa-
sion and many instructions were made by Dr. R.
B. Pratt, Pres. E. C.Clawson and E. B. Wells. The
meeting was adjourned with singing and prayer
which was offered by Mrs; Elizabeth Hill, Presi-
dent Relief Society. Morning session minutes
were taken by Miss Nellie Carlisle, afternoon
session Miss Belle White. Both these young wo-
men have been very efficient helps in the Primary
Association. Would there were more of the girls
in Zion would follow this example than there are,
to come forward and render such able assistance
with the little ones in teaching them the ways of
the Lord.


SUFFRAGE MEETING.

	The regular monthly meeting of the Woman's
Suffrage Association of Ogden City was held in
the County Court room, June 3rd, at 4 p. m.
There was a large attendance present and all ap-
peared enthusiastic over the object for which they
had organized and met—the franchise for woman.
Pres. Stanford presided,and Mrs. Sarah Richards,
chaplain, opened by prayer. The minutes of the
previous meeting were read and approved.
	Hon. C. W. Penrose of Salt Lake, was introduced
to the large audience and spoke on the privileges
that should be accorded women. The EXPONEFT
in its next issue will publish a synopsis giving the
principal points and subject matter of the speech
which was a most excellent and telling one.
At the conclusion of Mr. Penrose's speech
Miss Lizzie Ensign sang "The Brook" in a
charming manner, pleasingly accompanied by
Prof. L. D. Edwards.
	Hon. Lorin Farr gave a short address.
	A lively glee was sung by a number of young
ladies under the direction of Prof. Edwards.
	On call for members two new names were ob-
tained on the roll.
	Notice was given that at the next meeting Mrs.


E. B. Wells, editor of the WOMAN’S EXPONENT,
Salt Lake City, would address the society.
On motion the meeting adjourned for one
month.
E. PINGREE, Sec.


SERICULTURE.
	Several young girls were taught the reeling of
silk in Provo at the late Sister Beebee’s residence
and we sent quite a number of the skeins East to
be worked up. The Silk Association of Utah
Stake emigrated a woman, a silk weaver from
England to Provo; who wove dress goods, and
100 Handkerchiefs were made by her and another
sister in this settlement, and we donated 30 yards
of silk goods to the Manti Temple as furnishings.
We sent to the Chamber of Commerce a silk
handkerchief and reeled and spun silk which went
East in the Exposition Car.
	We are now having 50 pounds of Cocoons spun
which we intend making into dress goods. There
is a great need felt for the proper machinery for
manufacturing the silk, but the sisters of this
County do not feel like letting the silk industry
die out, though we realize all new enterprises
have to encounter many disadvantages, but we
still have faith in President Brigham Young's as-
surances on the silk question.
				M. A. TILL, Prest.
				JANE P. JONES, Secy.
	Brother Graves wishes made public in the
columns of the Exponent that Congress last year
donated $20,000 to the silk industry but confined
the reeling of silk to the District of Columbia as
experimental.
	He received this spring from the Department at
Washington, a number of Official Envelopes and
Circulars for distribution, also schedules to be
answered; enquiring the number of trees raised
for feeding silk worms, what experience and suc-
cess heretofore in raising cocoons, and the amount
of eggs wanted which would be furnished gra-
tuitously.
	Brother Graves made it known through the
papers and received applications for the same
from as far north as Cache Co., and to St. George
in Washington Co. Several persons in Provo
have taken eggs it being fully understood that the
market price will be given for all cocoons raised,
or if preferred they will be worked up on shares.
						M. A. TILL,
	President Utah Co., Silk Assocation.
Provo, June 5th, 1889.


WORDS TO MOTHERS.

	The key of the Human Heart is sympathy. The
divine sentiment given more abundantly perhaps
to mothers than to any other class of individuals.
Mothers what a grand thing to be a true mother
in every sense of the word. The mother! The
one who reigns supreme in a home: That guides
the stream of domestic life, and calms the disturb-
ing elements, which often arise in the best regu-
lated households. Her duty is to every member
of that household. To each she should be an af-
fectionate friend, a wise counselor, a kind re-
prover. Her responsibility does not end with her
care for her children, but like the brooding wings
of a dove must her ever watchful heart be overall
within her domains. The girl who washes the
dishes is not a mere machine. She is a living soul,
and though her lot be humble and she may have
been reared in ignorance the mother has a duty to
perform to her, and by a few words kindly spoken
may gain her confidence and perhaps lasting af-
fection, and both will be mutually benefited.
Show her that economy is not stinginess, by tell-
ing her, not to let the soap soak away in the dish-
water, dough enough to make a biscuit adhere to
the mixing pan there to dry and be scraped off
and thrown away, sugar be spilt on the outside of


the sack to tempt flies and be wasted, meal sacks
be left open, kettles be left dry to burn on a hot
stove, preparing of dishwater neglected until
wanted, thereby wasting time and fuel, and allow-
ing dirty dishes to stand to draw the flies, clothes
be left out longer than necessary to switch and be
beaten to pieces by the wind. If her moral nature
has not been trained to the strictest honesty she
may help herself to a few hairpins,the childrens’
garters or other little neccessities of the toilet, and
hot think she is doing wrong. Reprove her by
giving her a few hairpins or an occasional pair of
garters, and tell her to take care of her things and
neither waste her own or your things, and that by
taking care of our substance we can have where-
with to be generous. Tell her instead of all that
cheap satin on an ash colored easily soiled dress,
which she is soon disgusted with, to add the price
of the satin to the material and get her a better
and more durable substance. And instead of
sending for ready made, embroidered, bleached,
easily worn out underwear, to get good strong
factory and learn to make it herself, and buy an
occasional tfook with the balance, and instead of
wasting her leisure in running about with idle girls
and gossiping, to improve her mind with anocca-
sional half hour of good reading. Try to arrange
your work so that not only your own girls but the
hired girl can attend the improvement associa-
tion. This course will bring more intelligence
and a kindlier feeling into the kitchen.
	How often is it the case that the mother of a
large household will arise as weary as. when she
lay down, with so much work of various kinds
that it looks like a mountian. How then is she
to bring order out of chaos? First kneel in thank-
fulness for the protecting care of the past night
also ask for strength and wisdom, for patience and
charity which none need more than a mother.
Then when you begin your labors you will be
astonished at the rapidity with which first one
obstacle and then another is surmounted until
when evening comes you will have accomplished
more than you thought possible in the beginning;
and can again return your gratitude to the dis-
penser of all blessings.
	Oh wives! Oh mothers! Stand not on your
false pride which some mis-call dignity, but stand
on your humility which will not let you fall. Be
kind. Be patient, forgiving and humble. Meet
not your husband with hard hearts and cloudy
faces. No matter if you think you are imposed
upon. Better to be wronged than wrong others
Often when you think your affections are slighted
there will exist in the heart of your husband the
deepest tenderness for you. Perhaps he requires
much more of you than of other members of his
household. Does not our Heavenly Father the
same? ‘To whom much is given much is re-
quired," and “He who is faithful over a few things
will.be made ruler over many things.” Have any
great and noble ones gained an eminence through
luxury and ease? Was the path that our blessed
Savior trod lined with roses? Pray not then for
life’s burdens and sorrows to be removed. Rather
pray for strength to endure and wisdom to guide
you. For only through the vicissitudes of labor,
difficulties and sorrows borne with patience,
charity and humility can we gain that experience
which prepares us to be sympathetic, merciful and
just wives and mothers even queens to reign over
our posterity in righteousness.
							A MOTHER.6


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


ENVY. 

	Envy is not a habit; but might be properly
termed a disease, distempter of the mind, that dis-
orders all the faculties of it. It is not contagious
but proves fatal to the peace of mind and happi-
ness of those afflicted with it. 
	An envious person is one that makes himself
miserable contemplating the prosperity of others,


and lamenting his own adversity, his sole object
being to equal or excel his superiors; not so 
much so by exerting himself, or increasing his
own toil and ingenuity, to better his condition, as
by diminishing the merit due to the efforts of
others, while seeking his own elevation at the
cost of another's degradation. He so detests the
sound of praise in any one but self, that if by
chance one extol the virtues of a friend in his
presence, it casts a gloom over his countenance, 
starts the involuntary tear to his eye, and calls
into action, jealousy, revenge, falsehood, and the
lowest passions of the fallen man; and he really
suffers more in his mind and feelings, than if he
was afflicted with the most painful disease, or had
been seriously injured bodily. He is indeed a 
poor creature, and stands sorely in need of the
pity and sympathy of all mankind, from the fact
that his disposition will not allow him to enjoy
himself with anything, at any time, or on any
occasion. 
	Envy is not confined to any class of people, 
neither one portion of the country more than an-
other, but is found existing among the rich and
poor, high and low, and in every land and clime. 
It rages in the breasts of men and women, also
children, and has done ever since the world
was framed. It began with Satan, for after his fall
he could see nothing in Paradise to please him, 
and envied our first parents in their innocence, 
and therefore tempted them to sin. 
	It was this same spirit that caused Cain to kill
his brother Abel, and has been the prime cause of
action which finally terminated in the crucifixion 
of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ, and has led
to the martyrdom of all the prophets both ancient
and modern. 
	Apostle James says, third chapter; "Where
envy and strife is, there is confusion and every
evil work." And we know that the Lord's house
is a house of order, and that His holy Spirit will
not enter where strife and confusion exist; neither
is there peace and happiness in their midst, and
we are desirous of obtaining both, 
	Envy carries so much venom and poison with
it that it alieniates the affections of families, 
friends, associates, and even raises rebelion
against God. Therefore, we should see the great
importance of watching its approach in all its
disguises, and dislodge it before it finds a resting
or abiding place in our hearts. 
	Let us improve the time and talents giveu to
each, instead of envying that which is our neigh-
bors; for excellence is granted only as a reward
of labor, and the envious hate that excellence
which they are too indolent to reach. 
	It has been said and that truly, "The envier
envies all men and no man envies him.” Let us
banish every evil thought from our hearts and
cultivate the spirit of brotherly love and charity
in our soul to all mankind, by seeking to do good
one towards another, and building each other up, 
instead of pulling others down, that when our
life is ended it may be said of us, "She was in-
deed a true Latter-day Saint, humble to God, 
loyal to her country, kind to her family and friends, 
and benevolent to all."
				MISS AGGIE CAMPBELL.
	Manuscript paper 14th Ward.


RELIEF SOCIETY, ST. JOSEPH. 

	My sisters, it is needless to say that after an
absence of over ten years I rejoice in the
privilege of standing before you to report the
St. Joseph Stake of Arizona, where I have
been called to labor in the interest of the
Relief Society. I am proud to represent my
sisters in that far off land, as being faithful to 
the duties and obligations required of them, as
officers and members of that organization, in
providing for the poor, and administering to
the sick; not only in assisting the bishops re-

[Column 2]
lative to the wants of the needy, but in at-
tending funerals, clothing the dead properly, 
and have also been called upon to  speak
consoling words to the bereaved parents and
friends, explaining that beautiful, inspirational
hymn, composed by our late poetess, E. R. 
Snow, revealing the great eternal truth that
we have a mother in heaven; the Great Latter-
day prophet, Joseph Smith revealing the key
of knowledge to unlock the glorious principle
contained in that most consoling of all funeral
hymns, the pre-existence of spirits. The
Sectarian world with the bible in their
hands calling God, Father, consider it blas-
phemy to say we have a mother in heaven, and
that all the people on this earth are the off-
spring of our Father and mother in heaven.
		"Truth is reason, truth eternal
		Tells me I've a mother there."
	These exalting principles we endeavor to 
keep before the rising generation.
	Excuse me, as I was not called upon to 
preach the pre-existence of spirits, or to prove
the fact that there are women in heaven, but
merely to give a report of the St. Joseph Stake
of Arizona. 	
	We have eight organizations, with two
districts not fully organized into branches, 
numbering upwards of three hundred officers
and members. We hold our quarterly con-
ferences regularly with a good attendance. 
We hold our monthly meetings and take into
consideration the nursing of the sick and
treatment of disease. We are trying to hold
our annual fairs, not only to encourage the
Relief Society, but to develop the talent of
the Y. L. M. I. and Primary Associations, and
keep alive all these organizations. And I
can say for the young ladies, they are a credit
to the Stake; we attend all their conferences, 
and also those of the Primary Association.
	At our last fair we had on exhibition a fifty
pound sack of cacoons, raised by Stake Coun-
selor, Mary L. Ransome of the Relief Society. 
The Sisters also are raising some cotton used
mostly for batting; we have assisted in erect-
ing houses of worship in which we can hold
our meetings until we can build Society Halls. 
We have contributed $49.00 to the sufferers
in the Tennessee Massacre; $50.00 to those who
suffered under the rigid enforcement of the
Edmunds Law; $25.00 to assist a widow to 
emigrate; $300.00 to the Pima Hall built of
brick, which would be a credit to any of the
older Stakes of Zion. 	
	I am pleased to say that much credit is due
the sisters for contributing to the circulation 
of the WOMAN'S EXPONENT, and feel to urge
it upon them to continue, inasmuch as the
Associations cannot keep posted relative to 
their duties as officers without it. 
	President Layton attends all our conferences, 
and gives us that encouragement which tends
to the growth and progress of the organization; 
and being a man of experience in this church, 
has made a success of presiding, and coloniz-
ing, redeeming the waste places of Zion, and
encouraging the Saints in building up the
country. He is aware there is gross misrep-
resentation gone abroad relative to the health
of that Stake; it is one of the finest agricul-
tural countries that the Saints have had the
privilege of colonizing and that our Stake
reports will show. The mortality is not so 
great as some of the older Stakes of Utah. 
The Gila river affords as much or more
water for irrigating purposes than the Provo
river. I have seen a crop of wheat or barley
cut in July, and corn planted on the same 
land, each crop yielding fifty bushels and up-
wards per acre. Doubtless many of you are 
acquainted with Philemen Merrill and Wm. 
McBride, who are our patriarchs, together
with their worthy wives, they send kind re-
gards to their numerous friends.


Sister Syrena Merrill is located at St. David
110 miles, where, in connection with her hus-
band, they have not only acted as missionaries,
but as saviors to all who would give heed to 
their counsel.
	Suffice it to say that President Young
labored for twenty-five years; to colonize that
land, keeping missionaries there among the
Indians; and we expect to build up cities to
the name of the Lord, and fulfill the predic-
tion of the prophet Brigham Young if needs
be, with our own emigration, as our enemies
have not the power to stop them from coming
to the earth.
							W. EAST.

	P. S.---Since I came up I have not only attended
the sisters' meetings in Salt Lake City, but also
the sisters' conference in Wasatch Stake, and
afterwards was invited by the Bishops to speak
in the Sabbath meetings, in Charleston and
Wallsburg.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[column 1]
MEETINGS AT FILLMORE.

     On May 25 the Primary conference of
Millard Stake was held at Fillmore, commenc-
ing at 10 a.m., Stake President Mrs. D. Olsen
presiding.  The house was well filled with the
children and parents.  The exercises were well
given, and did great credit to the local Presi-
dents and those who had the training of the
little ones.  The Stake and local authorities 
were present, and President Hinckley in a 
brief speech expressed his great pleasure and
satisfaction with all he had heard; encou-
raged both teachers and children to go for-
ward in the good work, and prayed God to
bless them in all their efforts.
     At 2 p.m., the same day, the Relief Society
of Millard Stake held a quarterly confer-
ence at Fillmore; Mrs. E. Yates presiding;
assisted by her counselors, Mrs. Thompson
and Mrs. Robinson.
     The conference was well attended, and the
reports from the various wards were most
favorable.  All the wards were represented.
     President Yates congratulated the sisters
upon their zeal and good works among the
sick and the needy, and gave much excellent
advice, as did also her counselors, and Stake
secretary Mrs. D. Olsen.
     President Hinckley spoke highly of the work
the sisters were engaged in, and encouraged
them to continue zealous and faithful, that the
Lord might bless them.
     There was a large attendance, and the con-
ference was most interesting and instructive,
an excellent spirit prevailing throughout the
entire meeting.
     At the close of the Relief Society conference
at 4 p.m., A WOMAN SUFFRAGE MEETING was
held, the greater part of the women remain-
ing.  A preliminary meeting was held the
previous evening, when a committee was
appointed to consider and report upon a Con-
stitution and By-Laws for a Woman Suffrage
Association for Millard County.
     On Saturday afternoon the general meeting
was held and the Constitution and By-Laws re-
ported upon by the chairman of the committee,
Mrs. E. Henry.
     The meeting was opened by prayer, the
chaplain, Mrs. McBride officiating; the chair-
man of the day, Mrs. D. Olsen presiding.
The committee retired to decide upon the Con-
stitution, and during their absence, at the re-
quest of the chairman, Mrs. M. A. Y. Green-
halgh delivered the opening address and
stated the object for which the meeting was
convened.  Gave a brief sketch of the struggles
by women for their rights;  quoted Senator 
Blair and others, to show that it was not a 
favor they were asking, but simply the rights
of every citizen to vote for the men who make
the laws, under which they live and must

[column 2]
obey, pointing to the many noble men and 
women who throughout the civilized world
were laboring earnestly and faithfully for the
same.
     On the return of the committee with the
Constitution and By-Laws, they were read and
submitted to the meeting by the chairman, and
were unanimously adopted.  An intermission
of fifteen minutes was then taken, for the en-
rollment of members, to which 45 responded
and paid the membership fee, which had been
fixed at 25 cents.
     The meeting was again called to order, and
the election of officers proceeded with.
     The association is to be called "The Millard
County Woman Suffrage Association,"  with
Mrs. M. A. Y. Greenhalgh as President;
Mrs. D. Olsen, Mrs. Eugene Dalbrook, Mrs.
Melissa Olsen, of Deseret, as Vice-Presidents.
Mrs. E. Henry, Treasurer; Mrs. Lois Hamp-
ton, Secretary; Miss Mary Anderson, Assistant
Secretary, and Miss E. Henry, Corresponding
Secretary, with an Executive committee of
nine, so that all the settlements in the County
are represented in the officers.  The Constitu-
tion adopted is the same as that by the associa-
tion in Salt Lake City.
     Mrs. Greenhalgh again briefly addressed the 
meeting; thanking them for the honor conferred
upon her, and urging upon them the necessity
of unity, for in this only is strength, which in
the end must prevail.
     Mrs. D. Olsen read a very interesting essay,
subject, "Woman's Right to Vote and Her
Ability to use It."
     Mrs. Yates, in a neat and interesting speech,
spoke forcibly upon the subject of "Woman,
Her Labors, and Her Ability, Together With
Her Present Disabilities," and called the at-
tention of the meeting to the work which had
been done and is still being done, by noble
men and women in the cause of freedom and
of right.
     The meeting was a success, all appearing
satisfied with the choice of officers, and the
proceedings generally.
     The meeting terminated at 6 p.m., and was
closed by prayer by Mrs. D. Olsen.
				M. A. Y. GREENHALGH.


ADDRESS TO BISHOP ATWOOD.

	A few moments ago a friend said to me,
“Come and go with us to celebrate the seventy-
second anniversary of our esteemed Bishop,
the roast and feast are all prepared." Well, I
said, if I do, I must take my offering with me,
and here it is—a thought or two—and I was
reminded that forty years ago, in hard times, I
borrowed a wash tub of his wife; then a
stranger to me, but I have known them well
ever since. He was then on a mission. After
forty years of acquaintance what can I say?
I ought to say something, and that something
I am proud to say, is this; that Bishop Millen
Atwood has been everything that one consis-
tent person could ask of another. As a neigh-
bor he has been obliging; as a friend, friendly;
as a father, interested; as a bishop, he handles
your means as prudently and as carefully as
his own; and in fine we may say, that he is
one of the noblemen of the earth. Early to
rise, early to work, and his long life has been
a life of toil.
	His experience has fitted him for his bishop-
ric, hence, he is able to acknowledge the hand
of God. He was a pioneer to this our moun-
tain home. Here he turned the sod, and
prepared the desert to yield support to man.
And God heard his and others’ pravers, until
he sees the hosts around. Did he aid in this?
Yes, he in foreign lands, foot sore and weary,
delivered the message of the Gospel; still afoot
across the plains, he gathered the humble, and
chosen few.
	His chapter is too long to tell in a short


story, but we will aid him on with our love,
faith, and prayers, until he reaches a still higher
seat with the Gods, and is crowned with life
eternal. My friends, now while he lives, and
his head still whitens, may we follow his wise
counsel, and thus be prepared to continue our
acquaintance hereafter. And we pray that
his last days may be most happy and peaceful.
					DR. E. S. BARNEY.
May, 24, 1889.


MRS.  L. D. ALDER’S ADDRESS.

		TO THE W. S. A. IN OGDEN.

	Mrs. President, Ladies and Gentlemen:—I
am little accustomed to public speaking, es-
pecially on the subject of Politics. Our Asso-
ciation in Salt Lake has been organized nearly
four months; I think we have done well, con-
sidering the up-hill work we have had.
	The advancement and education of woman
is one of the main topics of thought and con-
versation among the thinkers of to-day. As
women, we have our family ties, our home
duties, and so many cares upon us, which make
us very weary. It has been said,
	“Man's work, is from sun till sun;
	Woman’s work, is never done.”

	We, most of us, realize this truism; but,
my sisters, though we have all this upon us,
we need not let our thoughts stay with the
menial offices of life.
	While we are in the kitchen, while we ply
the needle, our thoughts can soar as high as
heaven; and we can study upon, and even
think out a subject that engages our interest.
To us as mothers, is entrusted the well-being
(or the opposite,) of immortal souls. Beings
who are destined to live forever. Can this be
successfuly done by the mere care of the
bodily wants ? Must we not think of the
culture and advancement of the mind, the un-
dying intelligence within us? It is not for the
sake of voting only, that so many noble women
such as Mrs. Colby, Mrs. Stanton, Susan B.
Anthony, Mary Livermore and others, have
toiled a life time. 
	A propos of the fact of possessing the
ballot is the power it confers. Women are
naturally pure, noble minded, and would there-
fore vote for men who are also noble minded,
to enforce its laws. In fact, the laws should
be made by good men then they would be good
also. Women should take more inter-
est in the politics of the day. I know that
men say that politics is a dirty puddle in which
pure woman should never dabble. We cer-
tainly could not make the puddle more muddy
ihan it now is; and I believe it would be
cleansed of much of its corruption, by the
wise use woman would make of her influence.
	How shall we become companions to our
husbands and sons if we cannot be interested
in what so interests them?
	A very dear friend of mine, who has long
been a staunch suffragist, told me of an inci-
dent which occured in the life of a friend of
hers. He was a well informed, intelligent, and
highly educated man; his wife was equal to
him in all these attainments. They lived
happily as companions for many years. In
the course of time he was sent as a govern-
ment minister to France; as they had children
he thought it would be best for her to stay
and take care of the family, and of their
beautiful home on the Hudson. The husband,
of course, in the line of his duties, met and
conversed with intelligent men of many nations
upon the topics of the day, government, law,
and all the thousand questions that interest a
man in that position. The loving wife living
quietly in her home on the Hudson, kept him
informed of the welfare of the children, the
home and surroundings. When he returned


at the end of two years, he found that his
thoughts no longer interested his wife; he tried
to tell her of the many experiences he had, the
momentous questions which had been before
him, and the cares which had weighed upon
his mind; but she could not be interested;
her thoughts were narrowed down to the home,
while his encompassed the events of the world.
It grieved him to find that they were no
longer companions; and in speaking of it to
her, he said he had made the mistake of his
life in not taking his wife with him.
	We can take this incident and many that we
see around us, and shape our lives accordingly.
	There is a new era dawning for women all
over the world; and it behooves us, here in
Utah, where we have been disfranchised, to be
up and doing; and be prepared for the better
day, which is not far distant.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


SANDWICH ISLAND LETTER.

		TO MRS. ZINA D. H. YOUNG.

DEAR SISTER:
	Deeming it of sufficient importance that I
should give a short synopsis of our labors in
the Relief Society on the Sandwich Islands, I
submit the following.—At a general confer-
ence of the Church on these Islands held in
October 1888, I was called to preside over the
Relief Society of the Hawaiian Mission with
Sisters S. Y. Gates, Nellie Beesley and Libby
Noall as Counselors, since which time we have
done all we could for our Hawaiian sisters.
	At our late conference held April 6th, a
general conference of all the Societies were
called. I herewith enclose a statistical and
financial report in which it will be seen that
out of the twenty-seven branches now organized
we have seventeen only reported. We have
organized three more branches during the last
six months.
	Representatives from the different branches
made favorable reports showing that most of
the members were active and attended their
meetings. Were it possible for us to visit all the
branches on the Islands we could do much good.
We, have been enabled to visit the sisters
on this Island, with good results; teaching
them habits of industry; many of whom show
an aptitude, and are quite expert in braiding
and making hats, piecing, quilting, knitting
and crocheting, in which they take more in-
terest than in housekeeping, though we have
shown them, inasmuch as circumstances would
permit, the necessity of this. Heretofore the
husbands and fathers have done the most of
the cooking, but foreign customs, habits, and
industries, leave no time for the exercise of
former vocations; hence the necessity of Ha-
waiian women learning kitchen duties. I have
been very much pleased and gratified
with the success that has attended our labors;
there is a marked improvement both spiritual
and temporal among our Hawaiian sisters.
	The return of Sisters Gates, Beesley and
Noall, whom you will likely have the
privilege of meeting will give you more partic-
ulars concerning the progress of the good work
than it will be possible for me to write. With
kind regards I subscribe myself,
		Your Sister in the cause of Zion,
						LUCY M. KING.
	Laie, Sandwich Islands, April 16th, 1889.


A PROGRESSION BACKWARD.

	Ward McAllister, the famous field-marshal
at the Four Hundred, though he succeeded in
bringing himself and his following into merited
disfavor by his and their social flunkeyism be-
fore the centennial inauguration ceremonies, de-
serves more than passing commendation for his
blunt use of the terms “men” and “women.”


If the country will but agree to follow his
fashion in this sensible particular, there are few
who now despise his haughty exclusiveness
who will not be tempted to grant him generous
pardon for all past offences against the popular
democratic idea of what American society
should be. When he spoke of one of his antago-
nists as "that man Fish” he gave, unconscious-
ly or not, a striking example of the strength
and vigor of the English language; and when
he spoke of the “men and women” chosen for
the centennial quadrille, he jumped at once into
a popularity which was perhaps as unexpected
as it was unfamiliar to him.
	We are reminded every day of the effeminate
deterioration of the mother tongue in the use
of less healthy words for the plain old lan-
age which has beoome so dear. It is now
“gentleman” for every human being of the
male persusasion; “lady” for every daughter
of Eve beyond the age of pinafores; and these
two in all sorts of absurd combinations, which
only add useless syllables in order to make the
terms more ridiculous. If an editor and his
wife take a little trip his brother journalists
say he is accompanied by “his lady.” If the
editor happens to be a woman and she is es-
corted by her less prominent husband, the
notice would probably read, “Mrs. So-and-So,
and her gentleman.” If the papers are to
be believed, men have ceased to travel with
their wives now-a-days; in fact, men have
almost ceased to exist. We have a race of
gentlemen and ladies, some of whom perch on
the highest roost of society, while some clean
the boots and scrub the steps.
	If there is any virtue in anniversaries, we
ought to be reminded in this centennial year,
that pure old Anglo-Saxon was good enough
for the patriots and is still better than any
counterfeit. A step back in this respect would
be a step forward. If there is any word in
our language nobler than “man” it is “woman;”
and nothing can be better than these two unless
it is “husband" and “wife.”—Standard.


MISCELLANEOUS

	Forget mistakes; organize victory out of mis-
takes.—Robertson.
	Tears are often the telescope by which men
see far into heaven.—H. W. Beecher.
	God always has an angel of help for those
who are willing to do their duty.—Dr. Cuyler.
	Each feels himself happiest where he can use
his special gift, and labor in the calling as-
signed him by God.—K. V. Gerok.
	Reason cannot show itself more reasonable
than to cease reasoning on things that are
above reasoning.—Sir. Philip Sidney.
	To maintain an opinion because it is thine,
and not because it is true, is to maintain thy-
self, not the truth, and so to prefer thyself
above the truth.—Venning.
	In all things throughout the world the men
who look for the crooked will see the crooked,
and the men who look for the straight will see
the straight.
	Resolve to edge in a little reading every day,
if it is but a single sentence; if you gain fifteen
minutes a day, it will make itself felt at the end
of the year.—Horace Mann.
	None are so fond of secrets as those who do
not mean to keep them; such persons covet
secrets, as spendthrifts covet money, for the
purpose of circulation.—Colton.
	A white garment appears worse with soiling
than colored garments do when much soiled; so
a little fault in a good man attracts more at-
tention than great offenses in bad men.
	The most knowing man in the course of the


longest life will always have much to learn;
and the wisest and best, much to improve.—
Shaftesbury.
     Mrs. Fry, in responding to the address of 
welcome at the Texas State W. C. T. U. con-
vention recently, said: "Men and women must
stand side by side in the home, in the church,
in the schools, the colleges, in all trades, pro-
fessions, business and in the government.  The 
cry of help, help! is going up to God in a thou-
sand different pleas.  Oppressed women,
drunkards’ wives, women in houses of illfame,
the poor, starving sewing women, the prisoners,
slaves in vice, are all bound  by the chains of
sin and shame, which can only be broken by
the energy and zeal of true men and honest
women.  We want men and women to stand
side by side for good."  This is sound
doctrine, and seems to have been well received.
The far South is awaking.				Ex.


OBITUARY.

     Dear Sister.—It becomes my painful duty to request you
to publish the death of our esteemed sister, Anna
Jenkins.  She left her native country, Cownbuck Aberdare
South Wales; and arrived in Salt Lake City, August 20,
1868; she moved with her family to Cache Valley; after-
wards removed to this place.  She died May 13, 1889.
She was treasurer in the Relief Society here ever since
our first organization, and was a very active member,
a true Latter-day Saint, always on hand to do good; was
highly esteemed by all her acquaintances.  The members
of the Relief Society at her funeral, which was largely
attended, formed a procession in advance of the remains
from the house, to the meeting house, a block long.  Ap-
propriate remarks were made by the brethren.  She leaves
three sons, three daughters and forty-one grand-children,
to mourn her loss.
				Reapectfully,
					M. D. DAVIS, President R. S.
Samaria, Idaho.	May, 27, 1889.

COMFORT FOR THE MOTHER.

The following was written to Mrs. A.R. Shaw of Ogden,
on the death of her infant son.

	A flood of light the midnight filled 
 		And every sound to silence stilled,
	And right before the mother stood
		A stranger, mighty, pure and good.
	He looked into her anxious eyes
		Watching the varying feelings rise,
	And knew her soul’s calm, steadfast truth
		And all her blameless life from youth;
	Then answering her eyes of prayer
		He opened one white hand, and there
	Lay three rich jewels, Oh, more bright
		Than e'er had met her mortal sight.
	And ere she yet could understand
		He laid them in her trembling hand.
	“Study their beauty well" spake he,	
		“They are thine for all eternity."
	Lo! by their light new life began,
		New thoughts through all her being ran,
	While ecstacy thrilled through her frame
		The stranger gently spoke her name.
	And as she raised to him her eyes
		Took from her hands the precious prize;
	“The Father sent these but to show—
		What 'waits you while through life you go,
	Safe in the keeping of His love
		They shall be yours again, above.”
	Nor artist’s power nor poet’s spell
		The	words he taught could keep or tell,
	They went beyond the things of earth
		To where the mysteries have birth;
	Teaching why wrong and sin should grow
		They took from sorrow half its woe,
	Why the soul’s idols turn to clay
		And God’s own gifts are drawn away, 
	And how, though thorns bestrewed her path
		Her soul its guardian angel hath.

	Then, when the holy guest was gone
		A lesser light came, it was dawn.
	Like one alone yet fearing not—
		Silence—with sacred meaning fraught,
	God’s blessing seemed so near her there
		Life’s dullest burdens seemed no care,
	For that sweet sense filled all her soul
		And lightened, strengthened blessed the whole.
	Was this life zealot’s faith, ideal?
		No, by His blessing this was real,
	Stripped of the curse by childlike faith
		Earth wounds pain less, no trials scathe;
	And while the mortal still endures,
		Through deeper tests still strength assures; 
	Just as the heart of His own Son
		Bore suff'ring ’till his time was done,
[column 2]
	Not for wrong doing but to prove
		The soul God guards, naught else can move.

	The days went on and oft she thought
		Of the bright gems the stranger brought,
	Smiling to know their light should shine
		Her own ’mid splendors all divine.
	And strove that outward grace to win
		Worthy the grace of soul within;
	Yet only half their meaning knew—
		I hat splendors cast their shadows too.

	Heaven remembered her and gave
		The gift her riven heart had craved,
	A babe, whose wondrous eyes revealed
		Pure mysteries from earth concealed,
	Their silent beauty almost speech,
		Diviner truths and love to teach.
	They led her soul 'mid earthly scenes
		Forgettiug earth, where—downward leans
	The Unseen—so close to the veil
		It almost touches spirits frail,
	Worn faint with trial ’til the breath
		Through joy or woe—is kissed by death—
	And there her soul heard other things
		Just as the blind feel passing wings!
	So, day by day they lived until
		Life seemed so fitted to God’s will
	And prayer the sceptre; 'neath its sway 
		Real, grateful bliss filled each fair day.

	Day hath its end, its night; souls, too,
		Have lost their sun of life from view;
	No hands that lift, no voice that calls
		But vainly on the darkness falls
	Until His hour. However lost
		In sorrow’s wild, His love hath crossed
	The trackless waste, He knoweth where
		The mourner kneeleth in despair.
	O, mourning mother! Not alone
		Thy riven heart in sorrow's moan.
	Not bitter only, is thy lot.
		Thou hast not been by heaven forgot;
	No stain of earth had marred the name
		Of that fair boy thy soul did claim, 
	No fault had dimmed those beauteous eyes,
		Too bright were they for these dull skies;
	Thy boys—lo—God hath taken them,
		Thy jewels, thy soul’s diadem.

	Remember her, that mother pure
		Who didst the agony endure
	On Calvary's hill; thou know’st in part
		The anguish of her sacred heart.
	Thrice blessed is that home wherein
		The shad’wy wing of death hath been;
	Thou hast had joy, possession, love,
		Now, each of these, lifted above
	Earth’s marring or its robbing, bear
		Their witness to thy portion there
	Where blessings past and present all
		Upward ascending ever call
	The tireless soul and wearied feet
		With them before His throne to meet.

	The sweetest voices earth hath known
		Were those attuned to sorrow’s moan,
	And nearer sanctified are they,
		Where grief hath washed in tears away
	The idle thoughts of cloudless hours.
		Forgetting how, for sins of ours
	The only Sinless, bowed his head,
		And would, with Mary wept her dead.

	Not wind and wave in furious storm
		Snatched from thine arms his precious form,
	Not stealthy foe nor cruel flame
		Thy lovely babe had power to claim,
	Not blighted by long suffering
		Ere the loosed soul its flight could wing—
[column 3]
	Rejoice, O, mother! In thine arms
		Death could not thrill him with alarms;
	His last sweet words were unto thee.
		Thine the last face his eyes could see,
	Safe in our mountain home he sleeps;
		While the wide world still onward keeps 
	It’s headlong course of pride and wrong
		Warring ’gainst Truth with hatred strong—
	O! better in that little grave,
		With mission ended, spirit saved. 
				AUGUSTA JOYCE CROCHERON.
South Bountiful, June 13th, 1889.


HEPHZIBAH.
				II
	"Truth is stranger than fiction.”
	He grew quite enthusiastic on the subject,
and bye-and-bye said to his son, “it may be she
will marry her cousin Elisha and have the
homestead, and we shall see the village pros-
per again, for Elisha will have money.” This
was too much for the father, who, though
poor, compared to his eldest brother, was
proud, and replied bitterly, “No! he would
scorn a village girl, and my brother’s son
shall not look down upon my child.”  “Ah,
well, well, how foolish to differ about her mar-
riage on the day of her birth, strange indeed;
I cannot understand why we should thus drift
off into the future of one scarce yet initiated
into life; it may be all owing to her name.”
“It may be,” said Grandpa quietly “but it is
many a long day since I have been so aroused
as to dwell upon the future of any one, not
even my own sons, and to be thus exercised is
“passing strange.” And so the two—father
and son talked on; a channel of communica-
tion opened between them, that had been
closed for years. From that very day the old
Major seemed to have taken a new lease of 
life. The advent of the little stranger had
indeed created a change, and he watched her
from day to day, fancying he could see some
resemblance to that other Hephzibah for whom
she was Christened. As soon as she was old
enough to run about he insisted that she
should remain with him at the old homestead.
Much as the parents esteemed him, they could
not consent to such an arrangement, but
allowed little Hephzibah to visit at the old
home regularly with one of her elder sisters.
His love for the little one was so strong he
could not endure to be separated from her, but
kept her near him in the musty, old, deserted
house, until she was about as antique in her
manners as the aged veteran himself. When
other little girls were sewing “over and over”
or hemming by a thread, she would sit and
tell them long stories, and amuse them by the
hour together, and when they asked her where
she learned so many pretty tales, she would
answer, “In the old manor house,” talking to
herself. Poetry unwritten was in  her heart
and soul, and the realities of life were a perfect
blank to her ideal nature. Her practical
New England mother wondered what could
be done to bring the child down from the
airy heights, where she soared unconsciously,
to the level of the earth, and make her under-
stand the need of actual labor, in the work-a-
day world. Her hair, which was a mass of
bright brown waves, was never smooth, and
her white dimity aprons, made so neat and
nice, were soon crumpled and soiled; “generally


[Column 1]
untidy" was her mother's idea of the child's
appearance. At school, however, she progressed
rapidly, and was a favorite with her playmates;
but at night, she kept the household wide-a-
wake with her memorizing ballads and impro-
vising meaningless rhymes. Thus time passed
on; the semi-annual visits of the eldest son
from Boston continued as regular as ever, and
it came to his ears that little Hephzibah was
to be the heiress of the treasures and jewels of
his dead mother; it nettled him sorely, for he
had plead in vain to obtain them for his wife.
His father would never consent that any one
should ever possess them save those of his own
blood, and so he had thought his daughter
would some day inherit them, "Why not?"
he said to himself, "it is the proper thing and
in the direct line." He could not bear the
thought that the youngest son's daughter should
ever wear his mother's jewels. He was too
haughty to do more than barely recognize his
brother, and as for the wife and children, he
never deigned to notice them. One summer
when he came as usual he brought with him
his son Elisha, who, not knowing his aristocra-
tic father’s aversion to the villagers, romped
and played with his country cousins. The
grand-father was delighted, and inadvertently
said to his son one day when he happened to
see him with little Hephzibah, "That boy had
better marry his cousin Hephzibah and take 
possession here when he's of age." "My son
marry that country cousin of his? Never! I
would disown him. Let her marry one of her
own class." This quite enraged the old Major
who knew full well that her father was much
more honorable and generous than his city
brother; and not only so, but that the younger
son's wife was of a much better family than
the other daughter-in-law, and his hot temper
rose, and he replied, "She is too good for your
son, for now I remember who his mother was."
Angry words followed between the two, and
they were never afterwards reconciled to each
other. Little Elisha did not comprehend why
his father was not willing he should play with
Hephzibah, and persisted in being in her com-
pany, and begged his father to leave him in
the country, because he should be unhappy if
he must go back to Boston without his favorite
cousin. His girl cousin, however, was not
nearly so fond of him, in fact. she loved to
play by herself, and ransack old closets and
garrets, or wherever there were old things, for
which she seemed to have an unbounded  pas-
sion. She would sit for hours together among
the rubbish and dress her dolls and talk to
them as if they were conscious of all she said. 
She dwelt as much in a world of her own, as
did her aged grandfather, and they suited
each other admirably. Out of doors as well
as in, she enjoyed every nook and corner. The
orchard resounded with her sweet voice in
merry song and laughter, and there was not a 
tree she had not climbed. Her mother would
sometimes say, speaking of the fanciful child,
"Hephzibah has no more idea of eating and
drinking regularly, than the fairies and wood-
nymphs of which she is always talking and
dreaming." "Her ways are shiftless" she
would invariably add, "and that is what the
world will never overlook.” But the matter
rested there, and the child grew more unlike
other girls every day. She could not appre-
ciate her mother's fears and apprehensions
about the future, and while she was left to her
fine-spun fancies, and grandpa's curious stories,
with the old house and grounds to ramble
through at will, she cared not; an unsophisti-
cated child of nature, with a mind so rich in
imagery that all the earth wore radiant robes
of glory for her, and the star-bespangled
heavens, were mysteries she never cared to
have explained, but only to gaze on and wor-
ship from a distance. Little she dreamed that
in a few short years, life would reveal itself in

[Column 2]
a thousand glowing transports, and alas! in all
its cold and hard realities as well; but so it
proved.
	Ere long an event transpired that changed
all the current of Hephzibah's life. Her
father died suddenly, without a settlement of
any sort for his large family. The widowed
mother with three younger children, and not
one old enough to render much assistance in
the way of help or support, was more than
ever engrossed with the cares of life. Alas!
how heavy fall such burdens on women.
	The widow left so utterly dependent, with
ten children looking to her maintenance, took
up her burden heroically and never faltered.
Some said "The children's rich uncle will pro-
vide for them," But Mrs. H.—always an-
swered "No! we do not want his money; but if
I were a man with power to act, I would see
they had their own, of which he has robbed
them.” And this was true, for the eldest
brother taking advantage of the old Major's
idiosyncracies, had managed to obtain possession
of all his ready means, and used it in his own
business and then represented that he sup-
ported his father, and consequently all there
was worth claiming belonged to him alone.
One of the sons had died while quite young;
another gone, they knew not whither, for he
never wrote nor sent one word to comfort his
aged father; and the third lived a gay bachelor
life in the city of Boston. Neither of these
cared for the paternal estate, nor the heir-
looms of the family, therefore the eldest son
had full sway after his youngest brother's
death. He had always been annoyed about
his large family, and living so near the old
home, he feared his influence, but now he
breathed freely again, for he was gone where
he could no more trouble him about worldly
affairs. Inwardly he congratulated himself
that the "coast was clear," although he made
some pretensions to a desire to help the widow
and lighten her load of care.
	The widow really thought that in considera-
tion of the circumstances, the uncle would
take Hephzibah to Boston and educate her,
but no such offer was made, nor any hint of
such a thing given. The very next morning
after his brother's funeral, the rich merchant
bade them all good-bye ceremoniously, and
without a word of consolation, left for his own
luxurious home, knowing too that they were
entitled to a support at least, from the estate
he had charge of.
		      [to be continued.]

[Column 3]
meetings with only those she brought with
her. By the way, Indianola being so isolated,
the people greatly appreciate the too-seldom
visits of missionaries.
	Sister Felt's kind encouragement, seemed to
sink into the hearts of those who listened to
her. They appreciated her visit very much.
Little Vera Felt, who accompanied her, added
to the enjoyment of each meeting, with a
sweet recitation.
	We were received with the greatest kind-
ness and cordiality wherever we went. We
were greatly prospered and blessed on our
visit, and feel that the knowledge of God and
His ways, is increasing with our children.
His gifts and blessings are bestowed upon
them in rich abundance. There is a strong
attachment between the officers and members
of the Primary Associations.
	(We witnessed one touching, little episode on
our visit to Spring City. Their President,
Emma Robinson, had been bedfast for two or
three weeks. In the evening, the Primary
children, surrounded the bedroom window
and sang some of their beautiful songs. Sister
Robinson was moved to tears, and yet she
could not refrain from joining in with the
children in their praises to God, as she has
done so for fully nine years or more. After
the singing, many of the children crowded
around her bed, to press her hand and ask
God's blessings upon her.)
	Sister Felt expressed herself as being satis-
fied with the Primaries of Sanpete Stake, and
her visit to them. 
	Our manly little driver, Freddie Swalberg,
of Gunnison, did all that he could to speed us
on our journey. He took excellent care of our
team, and never missed a meeting. 
	Earnestly praying that our heavenly Father
will bless the Primaries in Zion, and all who
spend time, means, and labor for the advance-
ment of our precious children.
			We remain,
		HELENA MADSEN, Stake President,
		SARAH METCALF, Secretary,
	GUNNISON, June 11th, 1889.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


VISIT TO PRIMARIES.

	On the 6th of May, Sister Helena Madsen,
President of the Primaries of Sanpete, accom-
panied by Sister Louie B. Felt, President of
the Central Board of the Primary Associa-
tions, Counselor Lucy Berglund, and Stake
Secretary Sarah Metcalf, started to visit the
Primaries of this Stake, holding the first meet-
ing in Gunnison. There are 18 Primaries in
the Stake, which have been holding meetings
continuously, except where sickness hindered.
The meetings were exceedingly well attended;
notably in Fountain Green.
	The children in their exercises and be-
havior, manifested excellent progress; their
officers, in general, being energetic and solici-
tous for the welfare and advancement of their
precious charges.
	Sister Hulda C. Hjorth, President of the
Indianola Primary, furnishes a remarkable in-
stance of perseverance in the Primary cause.
She lives four miles from the meeting house;
in fact she lives in Utah County, about a mile
over the boundary.
	She takes a team and wagon and gathers up
the children on the way, and often has held


NOTES AND NEWS.
	Alice Stone Blackwell, edits The Woman's
Column, No. 3, Park Street, Boston, Mass.  It
is a weekly and but twenty-five cents a year,

	Every woman ordained to preach the gos-
pel, by any church or ecclesiastical organiza-
tion, is a legally suitable person to perform the
marriage ceremony.

	May 20th, the House of the Connecticut
Legislature passed the bill giving women the
right to vote on the question of the sale of in-
toxicating liquors.

	A proclamation was issued by Governor
Warren, of Wyoming Territory, for an elec-
tion of delegates to a Constitutional Conven-
tion to meet September 8.
	
	Mme. Carnot, as the first lady of France, is
as popular as she is beautiful; but both she
and her husband, Count Carnot, the President
of France, never use their titles.  She says,
“no title can be higher than that of President
of the French Republic, because that name
cannot be separated from the history of
France.
	Among all the competitors for the first prize
in the schools of San Francisco, it was won by
a girl, Miss May Ayers. The prize was a
ticket to the Paris exhibition and return. She
has sailed with her mother for Europe,
from New York, visiting Chicago en route.
This is quite a victory for the girls, and proves
the oft repeated assertion, that the brains of
girls are not inferior to the brains of boys.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


BIRTHDAY OFF'RING.

Wand'ring fancy backward turning,
	Lifts the curtain from the years;
Bridges o’er the long line travel’d,
	Mid its changing hopes and fears.
'Til we stand upon life’s threshold,
	Enter’d with unconscious tears.

There receiving royal welcome,
	Feted with the choicest fare;
Clothed in robes of finest texture,
	Wrought with skill artistic, rare.
Cherished, blessed, caressed and sheltered
	Ministered unto with care.

Thence thro' mornings bud and blooming,
	Green with gold and blue with white,
Blending into rainbow colors,
	Scenes of gladness and delight.
Until each revolving season
	Seemed a fairy land of light.	

Now beyond life's noon advancing
	Leaving childhood's rosy dawn,
And its glorious ideal,
	Gushing springs of magic charm.
Passed into the living real,
	Wearing off the bloom of morn.

With uplifted eyes we question,
	Has the real more of bliss,
Has the fount of knowledge given,
	Measure full for sweetness missed,
Off'ring stones for bread delicious,
	Hard, cold, facts for rainbow mist?

Still from sixty-five three hundred,
	One bright day is set apart;
One fair gem from each is numbered,
	And with special favor marked;
Held in grateful mem'ry ever,
	Treasured safe within the heart.

Like a peaceful benediction,
	Comes a day in "Merrie June,"
Lifts the soul to fields elysian
	All the universe in tune,	
All the discord of life's friction,
	Banished with its doubt and gloom.

Thus we blend in song and story,
	These anniversary days; 
Give to each an added glory, 
	Fresh with laurel green with bays,
That shall breathe of friendship holy
	In a psalm of perfect praise,

Fragrant with sweet incense ever,	
	As from Eden wafted down,
One by one these leaves we gather,
	Wreathe them into fairy crown,
Treasure trove, that ne’er shall wither,
	While the wheels of time move round.
							L.M. HEWLINGS.
Atchison Kan.


TRUE VALUE.

	What is truly valuable, or in other words,
what is of the most value to us? Some ap-
preciate those friends who have the most
money, because in the hour of need they
would benefit them, which is sometimes the
case, but not always. Did you ever see a
person who could not use all the money he
had, and more if he had it? Accordingly
people are not always charitable; for business
is business and must be attended to, at the
proper time; and the old adage is “Charity
begins at home.” We should learn from
this that it is well to become acquainted with
the business affairs of life while life lasts.
But knowing as we do, that life is short at
the best, we often cast a longing eye on the
other side, and ask ourselves, “How will we
find matters there?” and “What pursuit is
best calculated to prepare us for our exit to
the next state of existence?”
	We are told by our forefathers, both in the


Bible and Book of Mormon, that sound
principle, a pure life, and honorable dealings
with our fellows, is the right course to take
while we are here; as this course of life will
bring to us happiness both here and hereafter.
We are told that God made man in his own
image, and he possesses of His attributes.
	We can see readily that this is the reason
that persons who are upright, honorable and
true, are looked upon as being God-
like, and are deserving of our reverence and
respect. We have confidence in their word,
believing they will do as they wish to be
done by under like circumstances.
	This class of people I feel to call the
nobility of the earth. They may live in a
log house with a dirt roof, as I have found
them under pressing circumstances, yet they
were the true nobility of the earth, because
the principles they cherished, and the sacri-
fices they would make to aid and help the
oppressed, drew forth my love and admiration
for them; knowing that God our Father
claimed them as His own; for surely these
were God like principles.
	Is not this true riches, and of greater value
than all the precious metals combined? Yet,
if a person may possess both it is well, provid-
ing he has that spirit which giveth wisdom,
and enables him to be a wise steward.
	Is not this spirit then of the greatest value,
for without it, he is liable to misuse and
abuse these blessings, so that they even prove
a curse instead of a blessing, by yielding to
temptation, and then to folly, and finally to
crime.
	I think every thoughtful and reflecting
mind can see this clearly, and will eventually
know how to choose, and how to conduct
themselves, especially if we ask aid of this
good spirit, for we surely have help in our
upward course to heaven, and again in the
downward course; there is help in either
position.				M. E. KIMBALL.
	Salt Lake City, June 16th.


FAULT FINDING.

	There is nothing that destroys the happiness
of a home, so quickly and completely as fault-
finding. It is an enemy to peace and con-
tentment. Persons who indulge in this habit
of finding fault at home with those they love
the best, forget that home is the place to make
happiness, and to make this earth an Eden.
One fault finding person in a family can make
a home so miserable, that its members are
glad to go abroad to the neighbors to seek
that comfort which is denied them by their
own fireside.
	What young lady would wish to be num-
bered with the fault finders? Surely none!
Young ladies can do much toward making
home pleasant for their brothers and younger
members of the family, by being kind and
gentle, displaying their company manners at
home with their parents and brothers and
sisters. They can take a great burden from
the shoulders of their weary mother, by assist-
ing to take care of, and teach the children
lessons of good behavior, helping them with
their school lessons, selecting appropriate songs
and recitations for the Primary, getting them
ready to go, and in a thousand different ways
can a thoughtful, loving daughter, make the
burden light for her mother,and be a sunbeam,
a joy forever in the home.
	Girls, don’t find fault if your parents cannot
provide everything you think you want, be
grateful for what you have, and be willing to
labor for what you want. Not only should
girls be willing to work for themselves, but if
their families are in needy circumstances, they
should be willing, and thankful to be able to
assist them.


I fancy I hear some young lady say, "Oh!
it's a pity if my father can't keep me; I'm not
going to live out and earn my own living."
They are too proud for that, and think it a dis-
grace to be self-sustaining.  Girls who talk in
this manner do not look at life and labor in
their proper light.  Labor is honorable; idle-
ness dishonorable.  Girls who have an inde-
pendent spirit, scorn the idea of being depen-
dent, and consider it far more honorable to be
self-supporting, than to be a burden to an aged
father, who has perhaps, worn himself out
providing for his family while they were unable
to take care of themselves; and now when
they have grown to womanhood, and are
abundantly able to earn their own living,
why should they not be happy in doing so;
and if necessary, help to repay the debt they
owe their parents?  Do our parents owe us a
living when we are able to earn it ourselves?
Doee the world owe us a living?  I think not.
I think we are indebted to our parents for our
existence.  Jehovah decreed, that "man should
earn his bread by the sweat of his brow."  and
he further said in the Doctrine and Covenants,
that the idler should not eat the bread of the
laborer.  Let us not want something for
nothing, but let us labor faithfully, and
earnest labor will bring a sure reward.
							M. E. T.


FAITH.

	Faith is one of the firrst principles of the
Gospel.  "By faith we live, move, and have
our being."  We are told "If any are sick
among you, (you) should call for the elders of
the Church, they should anoint them with oil;
and the prayer of faith shall heal the sick, and
the Lord shall raise them up, if they are not
appointed unto death."  But there is still an-
other promise: "If they die, they die unto
me, and if they live, they live unto me, and if
they have committed sins they shall be forgiven
them."  This promise causes me to reflect a
great deal on this principle, and ask myself
the question—how it is that so many among
us, as soon as they are sick, send for the doc-
tor?  If the doctor is a friend, all right; for
the Lord said further: "If ye have not faith
to be healed, you shall be administered to
with mild herbs, by the hand of a friend—not
an enemy.  We read in the eleventh chapter
of Hebrews: "Faith is the substance of things
hoped for, the evidence of things not seen.  By
faith the worlds were framed, by faith Abel
offered sacrifice to the Lord, by faith Enoch
was translated, without faith it is impossible to
please God."  I could rehearse a great many
more evidences on faith, but this should be
sufficient to awaken our thoughts to the im-
portance of the cultivation of a principle so
essential to our life-work.
					SUSIE STEPHENSON,
	Manuscript Paper, Nephi.


Mrs. Z. D. H. Young recently made a tour
of Sanpete Co.’ in company with Mrs. M. A.
Hyde, and Prest. Peterson and wife.
	Clara Barton is still as active as ever.  Her
humane work was carried on in Paris after the
fall of the Commune.  When the Mississippi
overflowed its banks in 1884 she appeared,
laden with relief.  Her work in America,
additional to what has been mentioned, has re-
lieved the sufferers of the Ohio floods, Michi-
gan fires, Charleston earthquake, Texas drouth,
Mount Vernon tornado, and now the awful
calamity at Johnstown.  She is president of
the American Red Cross Society.  The motto
of the American branch is: "Relief in war,
famine, pestilence, and other national calami-
ties."
