SALT LAKE CITY, 					JULY 1, 1889.


LUCY  W. W. HAYES.

	A telegram from Freemont, Ohio, announced
the death of Mrs. Hayes, wife of Ex-President
R. B. Hayes on the 25, of June.  The lady died
at half past six in the morning, after an illness of
three days caused by paralysis.  Deceased was
born at Chillicothe, Ohio, August 28, 1831, and
was the youngest child of Dr. James Webb
and Maria Cook; her grandfather, Judge Isaac
Cook, came from Conn., in 1781, and her great
grandfathers fought in the Revolutionary War.
Her father served in the war of 1812.
	Miss Webb was married to R. B. Hayes, Dec.
30, 1852.  During the war of the rebellion, while
her husband was serving in the army, her home
was a refuge for the wounded, sick and furloughed
soldiers going to or returning from the front.
She spent two winters in camp with her husband
in Virginia, and after the battle at South Mountain
where he was badly wounded, she joined him at
Middleton, Maryland,  and also spent much time
at the hospital near Frederick City.
	Of her life at the White House, it is well known
that she distinguished herself by her cordiality
and amiability of manner.  She was highly com-
mended by temperance people, during her four
years sojourn at the White House, for never
allowing wine upon the table.  This fact alone,
shows what her strength of character must have
been, to succeed in carrying out her purpose in
opposition to the custom of society, and the
etiquette of the White House table.
	When we were in Washington in the winter of
1879, Mrs. Hayes was specially kind and consid-
erate, and tender in her feelings toward our sis-
ters, and free from prejudice on our question.
The writer was one of a committee of three to
call upon President Hayes, from the National
Woman Suffrage Convention, and present the
 Resolutions of the Suffrage Association. Mrs.
Sara A. Spencer read them and he smiled as
she read he was reproached for his neglect in
failing to menton the political, civil, or social
rights of women.
	After this part of the interview was
over, we succeeded in securing an appoint-
ment for an audience with the Presi-
dent on the Mormon question.  It was then
he brought his wife in and introduced her to the
party.  Mrs. Zina Young Williams accompanied
the writer and will remember as she does the
pleasant manner in which "the greatest lady of
the land," greeted us and the interest she mani-
fested in the matter we came to present; Miss
Grundy, the writer of society articles for the
Washington papers at that time (since deceased)
says "Mrs. Hayes heard the Mormon wives with
such earnest sympathy as to completely win their

[Column 2]
hearts.  She listened and spoke to them in a
sisterly manner that aroused in all present a feel-
ing of reverence for her noble,womanly nature.  She
invited the party to visit all the private apartments
and when they left sent them through the con-
servatories."
	When Mrs. Hayes visited Utah with her hus-
band in his trip across the continent she was also
very kind and courteous to Mormon women.  The
writer was one of the number who went to Ogden
to meet the Presidential party, while we were be-
tween Kaysville and Farmington Mrs. Hayes sent
and invited the ladies of the party to visit her in
her own car, and we all rode with her to the City.
She looked the very picture of elegant simplicity.
She was presented while here with a homemade silk
lace fichu as a souvenir of her visit, and a remem-
brance from the Mormon women of her attention
to them in Washington.  She was a noble and
generous woman, kindly in feeling and charming
in manner, she captivated all hearts.  We shall
always cherish her memory tenderly and we
deeply sympathize with the bereaved family, and
trust they may find such consolation as can only
come from One who heals all our sorrows if we
bring them to Him.


OLD FOLKS IN OGDEN.

	The Old Folks excursion went to Ogden on Wed-
nesday, June 25, celebrating their annual custom.
Their destination was Lester Park, where all had
been previously arranged by the committee,
who always take the greatest pains to make all
these nice old folks comfortable and have a good
time.  No pains is spared and really the old seem
to dwell upon it with fond anticipation each year.
There were about seven hundred from Salt Lake
and Davis Counties, and something near as
many more from Weber, Box Elder, Cache, and
Summit Counties. 
	It is quite an interesting sight to see so many
aged people together, and the Ogden people
gave them a royal reception.  The Ogden Brass
Band was on the streets when they arrived and 
gave them a hearty welcome.  The old folks
were conveyed in carriages and buggies to the
Park from the depot, and there everything was 
in order; long tables filled with good things and
decorated with flowers were all in readiness, and
young girls clothed in white waited upon these
happy old people.  The Plain City brass band
was also on hand, and played stirring music.
Everything passed of pleasantly, as it ever has
on these occasions, and the Committee must feel
gratified with their work.
	It is a beautiful custom thus to honor the vet-
erans annually, and make them feel they are
not forsaken.  Many of them are jubilant on these
excursions giving expression to their gratitude
for the privilege thus generously granted them.
The people give liberally for these festivities,
and the old folks are just as delighted with their
presents as children are with toys; and indeed
they are very often more for show than use.
There are many funny things said by the witty
ones, and the committee enjoy the jokes and
repartee heartily.
	However it is painfully sad and serious, as well
as enjoyable, and causes many solemn reflections
to pass through the thoughtful mind.
	The EXPONENT adds its congratulations, and
wishes for them one and  all a pleasant and
 peaceful voyage towards the setting sun of their
days upon the earth, and a glorious heritage here-
after, with an abundant entrance into the kingdom
of our Father, whose pity and care is over all,
and who will not let a sparrow fall without his
notice.  God bless the old folks forever, and
long live the Old Folks' Committee to devise
ways and means for their enjoyment each year.


LINES DEDICATED TO THE OLD
				FOLKS.

The dawn of life's morn, has long passed away
To the true, honored, veterans assembled to-day; 
Their sun is fast setting, in splendor to rise, 
In a far brighter land amid sunnier skies. 

Their springtime and summer, are now with the past, 
Both autumn and winter, approaching them fast; 
And the lonely still hours of life's closing day, 
Should surely be brightened with a kind, loving ray. 

O, then let us bless them, while they are here, 
Before they have passed to the other sphere; 
For they've borne the burden and heat of the day, 
In the conflict of life, their locks have turned grey. 

They've labored and toiled, in this once barren place, 
Built cities and towns that our valleys now grace; 
They've conquered the Desert, and made it to bloom
As Nature's sweet garden, in richest perfume.

They've stem'd the swift tides, when the billows rolled
	high
That crushed other barges in weakness, to die. 
Their path has been strewn with the briar and thorn, 
That leave sad traces of what they have borne. 

They've travel'd life's journey, almost to the end, 
Let us cheer their lone hearts as they slowly ascend, 
And bid the kind Angel to watch them with care, 
And tenderly guard, each silvery hair. 

For lonely 'twould be, were we sadly, bereft
Of the dear OLD FOLKS, and none of them left
To guide and direct, the wav'ring youth, 
In the pathway of virtue, of love, and of truth. 

O, treat them with kindness, and never once slight, 
Their gentle tones whispering, "do what is right."
Their words, we'll remember in some future day, 
When, one by one, they have all passed away. 

And let us remember, that we must all tread, 
The same path, traveled by the hoary head, 
And then shall we feel December's, cold breath, 
As we hasten towards the portals of death. 

May they who have parted with life's dearest friend, 
And are left with God only, on whom to depend, 
Find solace and comfort, in each lonely hour, 
For the most brilliant sunbeams follow the shower,

And may heaven bless the dear aged ones,
His noble daughters, and His loyal sons, 
And lengthen their days until life has no charm, 
Then bear them home safely, to rest from all harm. 
										A. C.


A REMEMBRANCE. 

MY DEAR SISTER WELLS:
	I linger in this beautiful place after my pleasant
task is done, to thank you for sending me Nov., 
1st, 1888, of the EXPONENT, I appreciate not only
your candid outspoken reference in relation to 
the book "Reminicenses of the Latter-day Saints,"
referred to during our transit from Provo to Salt
Lake, but I appreciate very much lines written on
"Trials and happiness," by E. B. W.  As well as 
those written by O. F. Whitney to his friend
Hebe, they find an echo in the soul and will be
placed among my choice secelections. 
	My enjoyment was marred at the Provo Con-
ference by being called out to attend to some
special business and then again to take the Train. 
Oh the changes a few years have wrought, the
many faithful Veterans of a few years ago who
have been called hence leave a fearful vacuum. 
Among the number, Ma Smoot, Sister Emily
Smoot, Sister Ruia Holden, Sister Margaret Cluff, 
Sister Jane Gee, and Sister Hester Beebee, and
yet the various institutions and societies of which
they were active members, move on in the hands
of other faithful ones, who have been called to fill
their places, Sister John with her Counselors M. 
E. Daniels and Sister Emily G. Cluff, now repre-
sent the Presidency of Utah County, Sister
Caroline Daniels, the faithful Secretary. Eleven
years have passed since she was appointed, hav-


ing never been absent from her post except once, 
although her health is now, and has been during
the past winter, very poor, still her indomitable
energy urged her out to fill her place, too feeble 
though she was, to go through entirely, she mani-
fested her interest in the work by her presence.  
	I now wear silk manufactured by the irre-
pressible Sister Beebee and her aids.  
	Sister M. A. Till and Sister Goodman with 
Sister Saunders and others whose names do not 
now occur to me, are still interested in that 
branch of industry.  I see Father Graves is still 
deeply interested, would have liked very much to 
have heard him through his remarks on that in-
teresting subject. 
	Thus it is in the magnificent plan of Jehovah, 
the work rolls on, and is not retarded by the 
summons to another sphere of any individual. 
			Very truly yours, 
							L. W. Kimball. 
	Logan temple, Cache Co., Utah, June 27, 1889


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


A TRIBUTE TO "GRANDMA SMITH:"

	Mrs. May M. Smith, was born Aug., 24th, in 
the year 1804, at Crieff, Perthshire Scotland. 
Died of old age on the 15th, June 1889, in Beaver
City, Beaver Co., Utah, aged 84years, 9 months
and 22 days. 
	She has lived the life of a true, sincere Latter-
day Saint, since joining the Church in her native 
land in 1854.  She came to Utah with the Hand-
cart company in 1856; suffering the want, priva-
tion and hardships, incident to that long, tedious, 
never to be forgotten journey, without a complaint. 
Encouraging friends and her dear ones all the way 
(she was then a widow with six children,) never 
losing faith in the goodness and mercies of 
Heaven and trusting implicitly in God for aid and 
protection through every affliction in life.  
	To me, from early childhood, she has been or
seemed,a very remarkable woman, one of nature's 
noblest models.  Being endowed with superior 
intelligence and having acquired knowledge that 
few with her advantages have attained.  She was 
a woman of unbounded faith and unimpaired prin-
ciple, humble and charitable in the most minute 
extent.  She was a thorough logician, a historian 
in the scriptures and biographies of great men 
countries and people.  Being a constant, intelli-
gent reader, and an honest, earnest thinker; she 
was a pleasent, agreeable conversationalist a just 
and noble friend.  
	"Grandma Smith," as she was lovingly and 
familiarly known in our City, has for the past ten 
years made her home in my father's house with 
her daughter, May B. Murdock; and up 'til within 
the last year, could read and write with a clearness 
and beauty, that few middle-aged people display. 
When she became stone blind her strength of 
character, her grandness of soul became most ap-
parent; that purity and excellence which is the 
birthright of the truly good man or woman here 
showed in all its simplicity, yet doubly great for 
'twas of an ethereal, intrinsic quality.  Not a single 
murmur escaped her at this last calamity, she 
meekly bowed to the will of One "who doeth all 
things well."  When I once said to her "grandma 
'tis too bad that you are deprived of your sight 
when 'twas so great a pleasure for you to read and 
write."  She replied "no Julia, God knows best 
and He has always been merciful and kind to me 
and mine, therefore, I will cheerfully and patiently 
bide His time 'til He calls me home." 
	She was at the Manti Temple last September 
with all her children, and had satisfactorily 
finished her work in the House of the Lord.  She 
was sick but eight hours, died as it were going to 
sleep.  She leaves one son Robert Bain, Smith-
field, Cache Co., May B. Murdock, Beaver, Beaver
Co., Mary S. Anderson, Rexburg, Idaho; Jane S. 
Coleman and Betsy S. Goodwin, Rabbit Valley,


Sevier Co., Utah.  All are true Latter-day Saints,
worthy representatives of a genuine mother, and
a just reward for the indefatigable efforts and
teachings of that grand old lady, whom all will
miss, yet, none mourn, for we know her rest is
peaceful and her mission well and nobly finished.
		Affectionately,
					J. P. M. FARNSWORTH.
	Frisco, Beaver Co., Utah June 22nd, 1889.
Logan Journal please copy.


MORGAN COUNTY W. S. A.

EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:
	On June 22nd, 1889, according to a call made
by a number of the prominent women, of the Co.,
a Mass Meeting was held at Morgan City school
house, for the purpose of organizing a branch
of the Woman Suffrage Association.
	On motion Mrs. Lydia Rich was appointed
Chairman, Miss Delia Smith, Secretary and Mrs.
E. Worlton, Chaplain.  Prayer was offered by the
chaplain, when Mrs. L. Rich stated the business
of the Meeting, and referred to the struggles that
have been and are still being made by our sister
women of the nation.  Said it was strictly a politi
cal movement and all are invited to join in peti-
tioning for our rights. The following women were
then unanimously sustained as officers of the as-
sociation.
	Mrs. H. C. Smith, President; Mrs. E. C. E.
Francis, first Vice-President; Mrs. Cleveland,
second Vice-President; (with Mrs,Addie Hunter
alternate providing she will not act as she was not
present at the meeting,) Mrs. Mary Ann Edding-
ton, third Vice-president. 
	Miss Delia Smith was sustained as permanent
Secretary; Miss Lovenia Francis, Corresponding
Secretary; Mrs. Jeanetta Hogg, Treasurer.
	The following women were then sustained as
an Executive Committe:  Mrs. Emma Clark,
Mrs. E. Wild, Mrs. Avilda Dickson, Mrs. Jane
Brough, Mrs. Louise Carigan, Mrs.Lena Edholm,
Mrs. Sarah Wardley.
	The Constitution of the N. W. S. A. was read
by the Secretary and adopted.  The By-Laws and
Regulations that were adopted in Salt Lake City
by the Utah W. S. A. were then read by sections
and adopted with one amendment viz. The By-
Laws and Regulations of the Morgan Co., Asso-
ciation.
	The organization having been effected the
Chairman announced that speeches would be in
order.
	Mrs. Smith being called responded in a short
address.  Thanked the ladies for the honor they
had conferred upon her, and hoped to be able to
retain their confidence.  Her heart was enlisted
in the Woman’s cause, never could see why wo-
men should be subject to laws she had no hand
in making, nor ruled over by those not of her
choice, that men and women should work together
politically as well as religiously and socially, said
there was a wide field in which women could be
profitably employed,thought they could materially
aid in correcting many of the evils of the day.
	Mrs. E. C. E. Francis then being called made
some very interesting and pointed remarks con-
cerning the object of the organization. Thought
the women had never abused the right of suffrage
and thought its being taken from them a great in-
justice &c.  There were several speeches made
and meeting was brought to a close, prayer by
Chaplain.
					MISS DELIA SMITH, Sec.


﻿			WOMAN SUFFRAGE.

HON. C. W. PENROSE AT THE W. S. A. OF OGDEN.

	I do not understand that the organization of
the ladies of Utah at the present time is to win

[Column 2]
back for themselves alone those political privi-
leges of which they have been deprived; but
to labor with all the rest of the women of the
country who are interested in the question for the
purpose of securing to women generally in the
United States those political rights which they
should enjoy as citizens of the United States.
The Government in which we live is supposed
to be a government of the people.  It is a gov-
ernment of the people, by the people and for
the people.  There was a time when there was
a dispute as to women having souls, but I think
that day is passed.
	I do not believe that any person who is at all
acquainted with the principles of the govern-
ment will dispute that a woman is a citizen.
Not only is she a citizen, but every woman who
becomes the wife of a citizen becomes by virtue
thereof, according to the laws of the United
States, a citizen herself.  So women have in
one respect a little advantage of the men.  No
matter how long she has resided here, if she
marries a citizen she becomes a citizen.  But
although a woman is called a citizen, and is
expected to perform the duties of a citizen as
far as she can and is subject to the law, still
she is deprived of any voice in the government.
She has no right to vote for an officer of the
government—unless, it may be, in some local-
ities they are permitted to vote for school
boards; and in one or two places for municipal
officers.
	In the Territory of Wyoming women can sit
on juries and serve as officers if their fellow
citizens feel inclined to elect them.  In the
Territory of Utah, ladies once held the right
of suffrage, but not the right to hold office.
Although women were entitled to vote, here
they were not entitled to hold office as the word
"male" is retained on the statute.  While as-
sociating with other members of the legislature,
I at one time attempted to get this word
erased, so that women might be placed on an
equality with men—not that I believe that wo-
men are qualified to act as Presidents of the
United States or judges or other executive
officers.  Perhaps some lady will disagree with
me in that respect, but I am open to convic-
tion if she wants to undertake to convince me;
but I am good deal like the Irish lady who
said "I am open to conviction but be my sowl
I would like to see the man that could convict
me."  (Laughter.)  It was not because I
thought they were qualified to hold office that
I desired to have the word erased; but I be-
lieved that there should be nothing on our
statute books which discriminated against any
portion of our community.  And that it should
be left to the good sense of the community to
select what offices women should be elected to
fill, if any.  I think every woman should vote
and have equal rights with the men at the polls;
and for this reason: that every woman is a
citizen, if she was born in the United States, or
naturalized, or the wife of a citizen, and she is
just as amenable to the laws.
	Now if a woman should steal (of course wo-
men would not steal, they are such nice crea-
tures) she is subject to punishment just the
same as the man; and so if she became intoxi-
cated (of course she would not).  If a woman
holds property she will have to pay taxes.  You
don't find any discrimination in that.  Under
our statutes women are permitted to hold pro-
perty.  A woman is an entity; she is a citizen
and can hold property in her own right.  Any
woman can hold any property which she ac-
cumulated before marriage.  It does not pass
from her to her husband as under the old com-
mon law.  As a woman can hold property in
her own right and must pay taxes upon that
property, she ought to have some voice in the
government as to the disposition of the revenue
derived in part from her property.  There are
a great many arguments—no objections—


[Column One]

against women voting; one is that women can
not be drafted into the army, and therefore
should not vote. Well have you not found
some men who would not make good soldiers?
They say a woman is not suitable for this, and
that she is not suitable for that and so on. A
great many of the arguments brought against
the women can also be brought against the
men. There are only a few men who are fit
to be leaders.
	There are certain leaders in the world that
have a peculiar force of character and in-
tellect. It will be so always in this world, and
I believe in the other. There is one intelli-
gence above another as there is one star above
another, and the greater will lead the less. All
of the objections which can be brought up
about women can be brought against men.
There is no reason, I think, why women in this
country should not have a right to vote. There
is another reason I have, and it is this: The
laws of the United States have given the fran-
chise to the colored people. I know a great
many women who are more fit to exercise this
franchise than the colored people. Go among the
people of the south and mingle with them and
see what a low order of intelligence exists,and
you will see thousands who have no idea of
the principles of this government who have the
suffrage, while thousands of intelligent women
are denied it.
	But if we will analyze all the objections
made against woman suffrage we will find that
there is no real basis for them. They say that
it will degrade a woman. How? They say she
will have to mingle in the crowd and be jostled
if she votes. Well does she not mingle with
the crowd when she goes to the theatre, in the
store, in places of business generally? Does she
not travel up and down the same thorough fares
and sometimes have to sleep in the same
sleepers? Are they demoralized by that? Not
a bit. They should have equal rights and
privileges. At one time there was a great deal
of ridicule when the women tried to study for
the profession of medicine. Now do women
become doctors? Yes, and no particular noise
is made about it. And so with every profes-
sion—we find women lawyers. Why, women
operate in Wall street in New York. Some
say it is unwomanly. Why should the woman
not have a right to make money if she has
the ability? There are many things that women
are adapted to and so with men. Now let me
make a little explanation with regard to this
matter in which there is  a great deal of mis-
conception, that is that citizenship and suffrage
are the same thing. It is a great mistake.
There are a great many citizens who do not
have the right of suffrage. No person can vote
except he be a citizen, though in some states
persons who have declared their intentions to
become citizens can vote, but as a rule no per-
son can vote unless he is a citizen. Qualifica-
tions, however, are different in different states
and each state has a right to form its own
local laws regulating the right of suffrage and
the qualifications of voters. That shows that
citizenship does not carry with it the right to
vote. Voting is a privilege, and not a right
and suffrage comes by a grant from the legis-
lature.
	I observed in reading some of the young
ladies’ journals discussing this matter, that
they are going to be superior to men. There is
to be a perfect revolution all over the United
States, the ballot box is to become purified,and
the government is to be purified, and all move-
ments which now actuate the people are going
to be obliterated. Everything is going to be
lovely, and, I suppose, to use an old phrase,
"The goose is going to hang high” (laughter).
I hope my friends in Ogden will not entertain
such nonsense. I believe that women will have
a right to vote, and that when they do there
will be no perceptible change. The principles

[Column Two]

of government will remain the same, and there
will be the same kind of struggle for office,and
the same kind of motives to gain office as there
is now. There is a prejudice and it is nothing
but a prejudice in the United States against
woman suffrage, and it grows out of that old
condition in which we existed under the old
common law, where the man was the head and
body of the house, and when woman married,
she lost her identity.
	I do not believe that women will be elected
to be judges of the Supreme Court of the
United States, or commanding officers of militia
or constables or anything of that kind. The
good sense of the community will select the
offices which they are the best qualified to fill.
Don’t you think a woman can be a treasurer
as well as a man can? There are women who
are fit to be treasurers, and I know of some who
handle the revenue of a city, county or other
government better than some men I have met.
I think certainly that the good sense of the
community would select such an office for the
women as they can fill with honor and profit,
but executive and judicial offices, if I had con-
trol of things, should only be occupied by men.
The female mind is not judicial. Women
generally jump at conclusions, and what would
sometimes take a man an hour they observe in
an instant. In judicial affairs it takes reason-
ing. You ask a woman any question and her
answer is “because.” They jump at conclu-
sions. They don’t count the steps—it is hop,
skip and jump; and often they light right on
the mark. There are offices which women can
fill and a good sensible community would de-
termine what those would be; and therefore I
think the time will come in the United States
that the people will have seen that as a govern-
ment of the people, all the people should have 
the right to vote.
	I want you to understand me clearly, if I
can make it plain to you: In your operations
to obtain the right to vote or hold office, you
should not make the claim as a right; it is not
a right, it is a privilege granted by law.
	I think there is another mistake made by the
ladies in their efforts to obtain this privilege.
That they are endeavoring to obtain an
amendment to the Constitution of the United
States. I believe in Democratic principles.
This is a government in which certain things
are delegated to the general government of the
United States, and there are other powers
which are retained by the respective states.
This is the government for the government of
the United States. A number of states, each
of which is sovereignized in its own sphere, are
joined together in the general government, and
have each given up a portion of their rights to
this general government; and one of the rights
which belong to them and which still remains
is the right to regulate the suffrage. The gov-
ernment of the United States derives its
powers from the Constitution, and it has no
powers and can exercise no powers only as they
are defined in that instrument; and under that
it has no power to regulate the matter. And
so when the women go to Congress and want
the Constitution amended,or the regulation for
voting to be altered, they are working against
the fundamental principles of the Constitu-
tion.
	Ladies and sisters, gentlemen brothers—we
are all brothers and sisters, Jew, Gentile, all
who dwell upon the earth are the children of
the great Father. We are all brothers and
sisters, for God created of one blood all nations
that dwell upon the earth. We are all
children of the great Father. So I say ladies
and sisters, gentlemen and brothers—in your
efforts move upon a sensible line. Do not fol-
low the multitude to delusion or folly. A great
many things are being done and said. You
who are organized need not follow the ladies of
the United States into paths of folly and non-

[Column Three]

sense. You want to study principles of gov-
ernment—and that is one of the important
things I think this association should enter
into. I think you will get a greater interest in
your meetings if they do this. Take up prin-
ciples of government, municipal government,
county government, state government, and
then national government. You ladies should
study these things. And another thing if they
never understood these things how can the
mother teach her boy the true principles of
this government, if she does not understand
them herself. Every boy has a political right
to be President of the United States, and who
knows but your boy may become president?
You can’t tell. Curious changes take place.
You want to understand these things so as to
teach them to your children. We should
teach our children to be obedient to the law.
No community can have order without law—
without law there is no peace, and without
peace there is no happiness, and the foundation
of happiness is laid in law.
	The great difficulty with a great many
parties is that they are not subject to restraint.
They want to run wild. There is a spirit of
independence we must not indulge in our child-
ren. The principles of law should be under-
stood by the mother so that she may teach
them to her son, and that they may have a
disposition to understand what law is. When
you come together let some ladies whose minds
run in that direction take up some principle of
self-government and explain it for the benefit
of the others. We should study these prin-
ciples, and if we find any weak places, find out
how they can be shunned. We want to build
up this government, not tear it down. We
want to establish a good government.
	When you get the ballot use it wisely—
not as a little bit of paper of no worth, but as
something of great inportance. Many of the
men in the states go to the polls like cattle
driven by men. I have heard of men being
carried to the polls, a ticket thrust into their
hands and held until they had given it to the
judge and then hustled out. I do not think wo-
men will do this, but I can’t tell. I think they
will try to understand what they are voting for,
and I think they will vote for good and wise
men. That they will find out the best men for
offices—find out the persons best fitted to fill
them—and then vote for honest men, wise men
and good men. By way of preparation for
that, learn to understand priciples of law and
the principles of government of each little
town, of localities, of the territories, of the
states, of the nation, and then go on if you have
time enough to learn inter-national law, the in-
tercourse between one nation and another.
Use your intelligence for good, virtue, order,
peace and the maintenance of good order, and
the establishment of good government. Women
have a good deal of power which is unseen.
Woman’s power does not consist in rule and
dominion; but in that quiet domestic influence
which she exercises at home. If you do not
get the suffrage, your position is just as essential
as the position of men. You can never be men
if you try to be. There is no use trying. I
have heard women say “O I wish I was a man
only for a few minutes.” There is as much
chance to do good, as you are. Neither can
get along without the other, but the Lord has
designed that they should go together. Nature
has pointed out the path, and we see it in every
form of creation, in the fruits, vegetables, trees,
and in everything there is male and female.
	If there is anything which you are deprived
of, struggle for it. Ask for it, it is your privi-
lege. Do not claim that you are so much
better than the men; but contend for the prin-
ciples of sound reason, contend for the privi-
leges you ought to have as American citizens;
and I tell you, if you can only convert your
own sex you can secure the rights of suffrage.


How many ladies in Weber County are in-
terested in this move? Convert your sex first.
It is only the minority of women that are seek-
ing to obtain the suffrage. I shall be pleased
to see the day when you shall have this privi-
lege granted you. May the day soon hasten, I 
shall hail it, and shall be very thankful to see 
women placed on an equality with men.


﻿WOMANS EXPONENT.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


R. S.,Y. L. M. I. A., AND P. A. REPORTS.

		SAN JUAN STAKE.

	The quarterly conference of the Relief So-
ciety of this Stake, was held at Bluff, May 26,
1889; President Jane M. Walton presiding.
Singing "My God The Spring of All my
Joys."  Prayer by Counselor Wm. Halls.
Singing, "Zion Stands by Hills Sorrounded,"
Roll called.  Stake offioers present, five; local
officers, seven.  Minutes of last conference
read and approved.
	President Jane M. Walton made brief re-
marks.  Said she felt at home with the sisters;
prayed God to give her strength to fill her
honorable position; thought our conferences
were to strengthen us; was sure if we lived up
to the duties required of us we would become
great women.  The general and local officers
of the Relief Society were sustained.
	Presidents of the different branches gave
their reports which showed a marked improve-
ment in most places, which was most gratify-
ing.
	President Kristen Nielson reported the Bluff
Ward Society; also bore her testimony and
hoped to be faithful and true.
	Counselor M. M. Halls of Mancos felt she
had quite a responsibility in reporting all
the associations of that place, but was willing 
to do all in her power.  Said the Society was
not in so flourishing a condition as at last con-
ference, owing to the late disease that had
visited their town, namely, smallpox.
	Abigail Stevens said she had not been home
since last fall conference, her home being in
Burnham; but had corresponded regularly
with the Secretary.  The Society held meetings
regularly; they owned a stand of bees; the
teachers are doing a good work.  "We should
be careful how we train our children; teach
them not to interfere with that which does not
belong to them.  We should always be exem-
plary before them and guard our tongues, and
be careful what we say.  We should always
take a course to gain confidence.
	Counselor Daniel Allen reported the Moab
Ward Relief Society; said he had visited the
Society; they had done very well considering
inconvenices, the average attendance being five
members; they have some little property.
	President Jane M. Walton reported the
Monticello Society; the sisters felt to take hold.
Hoped the time would come when the Society
could be fully organized.  Wanted the
branches to store up grain.
	Counselor Martha Hammond addressed the
conference.  Related her late visit to the gen-
eral conference in Salt Lake City.  Knew the
faith and prayers of the sisters would give us
strength when trying to speak; said the sisters
should not speculate with their grair. She
had been given a mission to get the sisters to
take the EXPONENT, there are instructions in
that paper that we need.  Prayed God to bless
all.
	Counselor Josephine C. Wood, said she felt
well in the Gospel; felt we should be humble
and prayerful,and try and overcome our weak-
nesses; knew if we  were always thus, we 
would be able to stand the trials we are called
to pass through with more patience.  Wanted
to do her whole duty, and hoped she would
always be found on the side of right.
	Bishop Jens Nielson of Bluff, felt to endorse

[Column 2]
all the sisters had said.  Thought the
brethren should join the sisters in train-
ing  the children and carrying out their
praise-worthy ideas.  Hoped the instructions
given during this conference would sink
deeply into the hearts of all; asked God to
bless the society in all their undertakings.
	President J. M. Walton made some closing
remarks; hoped the sisters would all attend
the next conference.  Exhorted the brethren
to go and take them.  Spoke of the duties of
mothers to their children, and the amount of
influence a mother has over her children.
	President F. A. Hammond felt there was a
good influence prevailing.  Said the instruc-
tions were excellent at the late conference in
Salt Lake.  There is a movement on foot to
unite the sisters with the Woman Suffrage
society that we might gain strength.  Endorsed
the remarks in regard to taking the EXPO-
NENT; the sisters should take, and thoroughly
read it.  Urged all the society to get good
books; felt that it could not be impressed too
strongly.  Wanted the sisters to teach tithing.
Mothers! teach your girls to be help-meets;
find something for them to do; idleness comes
from an evil source.  Teach them to be self-
sustaining, and not wait for a man who is
able to keep them in ease and luxmry.
	Conference adjonrned for three months, to
meet at Moab.  Singing, "Now We'll Sing
With One Accord."  Prayer by Counselor
Wm. Adams.
	SARAH JANE ROGERSON, Stake Secretary.

		SNOWFLAKE STAKE.
	The Relief Society Conference of this Stake
was held at Snowflake, June 1st, 1889.  Presi-
dent Emma S. Smith, presiding.
	After a few opening remarks by President
Smith, Sister Mary J. West addressed the
meeting, and spoke of the time when the Snow-
flake Society was organizad, and the course
they had taken to accumulate property.  The
success that had crowned their efforts thus far,
she considered was due to the faithfulness of
the members; and for the peaceful feeling that
existed among them, considerable credit should
be given the head teacher, Sister Jemima W.
Smith.
	Sister Lucy H. Flake said: "The first thing
for us to do, is to do the will of the Lord, and
He will make up to us all we lack.  All of us
have missions to perform, and we should seek
the Holy Spirit to direct us."
	Sister Ann Brewer felt that the young peo-
ple have great blessings and privileges, greater
perhaps than the older ones, as all their pros-
pects are before them.  Urged them to attend
all places of instruction, and improve their
time in learning things of value, as it will
enable them to do a vast amount of good.
	Sister Fannie J. Willis spoke a few mo-
ments; was thankful for the return of con-
ference days, and the privilege of seeing faces
not seen for a long time.
	Counselor Sarah Driggs then addressed the
meeting, on the subject of taking care of the
means in the Society.
	Amelia Cardon bore her testimony to the
truth of this work, and said it will develop all
the good there is in us.
	Counselor Emily J. Lewis said: "We are
living in a day when people are tried, but
whatever may try us let us never deny any
principle of the Gospel, for they are all pure.
Let us try to be one, let us cease to do evil and
seek to keep the commandments of God, and
He will bless us.
	Sisters Sarah Cope, Anna I. Farnsworth,
Mary Savage and Martha Stratton also made
remarks.	
	Frances R. Thompson, Susan E. Allen,
Mary Peterson, Mary D. Farnsworth and

WESTMINSTER ABBEY.

			BY J. C. KITTREDGE.

CHOIR AND CHAPEL OF EDWARD THE CON-
				FESSOR.	
	With reverent feet one ascends the steps
leading to the ancient choir, which contains
very much that is interesting; although the
objects are covered with the dust of ages.
Hence one looks upon the chantry of Henry
V, which is rather isolated. The effigy of the
king (which is of wood), is badly disfigured,
the head being entirely gone. Above are the
identical saddle, helmet, and shield used at the
battles of Crecy, Poitiers, and Agincourt. The
Westminster boys as they have sat at service on
Sundays, have looked up at the shield and
helmet of the departed warrior. We hope the
view inspired heroism and nobleness. The
helmet twice saved the life of Henry at Agin-
court. The richly sculptured arch was greatly
admired by Fiaxman. Upon it is represented
the coronation of Henry V, as it took place in
this very church, by Arundel, Archbishop of
Canterbury, and Henry Beaufort, uncle of the
king. Over the canopies are badges of ante-
lopes and swans, from his mother, co-heiress of
the Bohuns. The same animals are likewise
represented on the cornice, chained to a tree,
on which is a flaming cresset. This latter was
Henry’s badge, and signified that he would en-
deavor to lead his people in honor and virtue;
also the changes in conduct from prince to
king. The chantry is a distinguished piece of
work. This figure, H (in the ornamentation),de-
notes two founders, and two kingdoms which
he had united, France and England. The
sculpture describes the coat and other accom-
paniments, with peculiarities of the man, and
the following selection:	
	“Vaulted with love unto us.”
	“As if an angel dropped down from the


[Column One]
clouds, to witch the world with noble horse-
manship.”
	Of the statue, which was in English oak, but
a fragment now remains. It was bound in
silver gilt, with head of solid silver.  Two
golden teeth were plundered in the reign of
Edward IV, and all the silver was taken at the
time of the dissolution. By some estimates
Henry V was the best English sovereign to his
time. He certainly achieved great things in
his short career of thirty-four years. He gave
the praise chiefly to God. The lines on his
tomb are indicative of the esteem in which he
was held by the people. Here are some of them:
	“All England mourned.”
	“Hung be the heavens in black; yield day
to night.”
	“King Henry V, too famous to live long.”
	“England ne’er lost a king of so much
worth.”
	He was the principal instrument in bringing
about the annexation of France to England.
He died in the former country. His body was
brought over with great pomp, processions
greeting it on the way. Shakspeare (by far
the greatest of English historians) has made
the life of the king very clear to us. As we
looked on we recalled the words—“Men whose
limbs were made in England.”

TOMBS OF EDWARD III AND QUEEN PHILLIPA.

	These are fine tombs, brass effigies on top,
which are said to be very correct likenesses.
The queen bore her husband many children, a
number of whom died before their parents;
among them the promising Black Prince.
When near her end, the good queen called her
husband, who tenderly loved her, to her bed-
side and implored him to continue the same
truly regal life, after her death, which he had
hitherto led. She also requested that he might
be buried beside her. As an evidence of the
unfaithfulness of man it is reported that at a
tournament in Smithfield the royal widower
paraded with a mistress, forgetting the injunc-
tions of his dying wife. Under the Edwards
England was great; unity having been brought
about by conquest. Beyond is the simple altar
tomb of Edward I (Longshanks). At one
time there was some doubt as to whether his
remains were really desposited there; but not
long since, all conjecture was removed by an
examination which was made; when, to the
great amazement of those who looked,the body,
which had been embalmed, was found in a
state of excellent preservation; measuring more
than six feet in length showing that Edward
well merited the appellation, “Longshanks.”
On the tomb are brass effigies, representing
Edward I and Queen Eleanor, which are said
to be correct likenesses. She was a most ex-
cellent princess; and at her death Edward
mourned her loss. To show his devotion he
erected nine crosses to her memory at the towns
where the body rested during the journey to
London. Charing Cross was one of them.
That great king subjugated Scotland and
Wales. Edward possessed vehement energy
and a strong, conquering spirit. He wished
his body to be carried before the army until
Scotland should be completely subdued.
	The tombs of Richard II and his queen are
also in this enclosure.

SHRINE OF EDWARD THE CONFESSOR.
	
	In this section is the oldest memorial in the
abbey—that of its founder, Edward the Con-
fessor. It is an ancient-looking tomb; and
hither Roman Catholic pilgrims come to this
day. Old armorial bearings are seen upon the
sides of the tomb. This chapel was the holy of
holies of Edward the Confessor. The pave-
ment glows with peacock hues. The coffin is
of stone, iron bound, with a shrine of marble
and mosaics. The tomb was injured at the
dissolution, but was restored by Mary I. It

[Column Two]
was again defaced by the falling of scaffolding,
at the coronation of James II; it was robbed
also at that time. From an elevated position
one can look into the urn. To the left of the
tomb of the confessor is that of Edith, his
queen, the daughter of Earl Godwin, sister of
Harold. In this place the Stone of Scone and
the Scottish regalia were offered. At one time,
behind the shrine were the relics of Saint
Edward, as the confessor came to be called.
There was also a tooth of Saint Athanasius, a
stone with a foot-print of the Savior and a phial
of precious blood. On the screen a fantastic
legend is told of the confessor. The designs
are as follows:
	First—Bishops are showing fealty to the
child.
	Second—Represents the birth of Islip.
	Third—The coronation of the confessor,
1043. 
	Fourth—He sees the devil dancing on Han-
gelt, and abolishes it.
	Here we have a bit of practical history. The
Dangelt or Danish tax had become oppressive,
and Edward did a good thing for his people in
abolishing it. One sometimes sees much mean-
ing in those old, fantastic works of art; they
make history more intelligible to us moderns.
To return to the description.
	Fifth—Describes a scullion warned who has
been stealing from the treasure chest. This
was perhaps a satire on some high official.
	Sixth—he sees the Savior in a vision on an
altar where he is about to receive the sacra-
ment.
	Seventh—He has a vision of the king of
Denmark who is drowned on his way to
England.
	Eighth—Harold and Tostig quarrel at the
king’s table, prophetic of future disagree-
ments.
	Ninth—The confessor in the midst of his
courtiers has a vision of the seven sleepers of
Ephesus, who turn from right to left.
	Tenth—The confessor meets Saint John and
gives him a ring.
	Eleventh—The blind restored to sight by
water in which the confessor had washed. That
superstition may have been the parent of the
one which attributed to the king the virtue of
curing the king’s evil or scrofula, by touching
the patient, which was believed in as late as
the time of Queen Anne.
	Twelfth—Saint John sends two pilgrims to
the confessor to take back the ring, and to say
that within six months he would meet him in
paradise.
	Thirteenth—Pilgrims deliver message and
ring to the king.
	Fourteenth—Edward, warned of his death,
completes the dedication of the abbey.
	With certain suggestive truths in those de-
signs are given a few superstitions, which re-
mind us of the idolatry displayed for Becket
at Canterbury. The observer can learn from
them what the decline in superstition has been.
Let us imagine an English monarch to-day be-
lieving such things? It is true that the world
moves in some directions.
	The shrine of Edward the Confessor forms
an appropriate center of this most ancient
center of the edifice. Not far from the shrine
of Edward the Confessor is the fine tomb of
Henry III. An inscription tells of his long
reign of fifty-six years. The statue is of gilded
brass, representing the king wearing a long
mantle. His son, Edward I, brought from
abroad fitting stones for it.
			[To be continued.]


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


IN MEMORIAM.

	In memory of Sister Elizabeth Stocking, who died April
9th, 1889, at South Jordan.
	Deceased was the daughter of the well known Henry
Arnold of Salt Lake City, who emigrated from England


when she was about two years old, her mother having died
previous, born June 19th 1847.	
	We the Sisters of the Y. L. M. I. A. unanimously adopt
the following resolutions to the memory of Sister Elizabeth
Stocking.	
	Whereas it has pleased bur Heavenly Father to call
from our midst by the hand of death our beloved Sister
and co-worker, therefore be it,
	Resolved that we recognize in the death of our Sister the
loss of a true friend and former counselor in the Associa-
tion. 
	Resolved that we esteem her goodness and uncomplain-
ing endurance, may we emulate her noble example as
sisters of the Association.
	Resolved that these resolutions be put on record and
that we condole with the family who are called to mourn
the loss of a kind and loving mother, eight children being
left orphans.
	Rosolved we send a copy to the family also one to the
EXPONENT for publication.
							MARIA HOLT,
							SOPHIA WARDLE,
							NAOMI V. OLIVER,
							THURZA CASE,
										Committee.

MEMORY OF THE SEA. 

In the midnight hour, a memory 
	Swept like music o'er my soul 
As I stood in silent reverie, 
	Where the surging billows roll;—
Minor music, sad and sorrowing, 
	Full of trembling, full of tears;  
Ever like the ocean's murmuring, 
	Bringing back the tide of years.  
Telling of the long forgotten 
	In the cycles of the past, 
Of the nations crushed and broken 
	In the world's great holocaust, 

As I listened so entrancing 
	Was the music of the sea; 
That I fancied mermaids dancing 
	To the midnight minstrelsy.
And a thousand harpstrings quivering, 
	Sobbing in the midnight sea; 
And my broken heart-strings shivering 
	As sad memories came to me. 
Had I caught the inspiration 
	Of the music deep and strong 
That had moved my soul's wild passion 
	Was it but a syren’s song? 
O, such music, weird and mournful, 
	As the night-wind swept along, 
And the shattered notes so painful, 
	Making discord in the song.  

How far off the dreamy vision 
	That these memories brought to me, 
As I strained my ear to listen 
	To the murmuring in the sea. 
Far down where the sea weeds whisper 
	To the corals and the shells; 
But they keep the secret ever, 
	Roar or echo never tells. 
But the human heart's emotion, 
	Answers to the sad refrain, 
And the ceaseless moan of ocean, 
	Brings a grandeur fraught with pain. 

While the wild waves in commotion, 
	Sweeping out unto the shore; 
Bounding billows, restless ocean, 
	Echoing forevermore. 
And the ever constant beating 
	'Gainst the rocks that hemm'd the sea, 
Where the winds in fury meeting. 
	Dashed them backward ruthlessly. 
So our human hopes are driven, 
	Recklessly tossed to and fro, 
And our strongest ties are riven—
	Rent asunder by a blow. 
Ever heaves the restless ocean, 
	With its hidden mystery,
Sleeping' in its surging bosom,  
	Until time shall cease to he. 
							E. B. W.


GOOD COMPANY.

	It is very necessary for us to keep good
company, if we wish to bear a good name. For 
we are generally judged by the company we
keep.  The happiness of our lives depends to a
great extent upon our training in that direc-
tion.  We should seek the society of our
superiors, and set a good example before our
younger brothers and sisters, and all with whom
we may associate.  Habits of this kind once
formed will cling to a boy or girl through life,
and are of great value to them.  Example must
go hand in hand with precept.  If a person
once falls into bad company and becomes wild
and reckless, he is very apt to lose the spirit
of the Gospel and go down to destruction. On
the contrary if we seek good society, we will
grow up to be good men and women.  It is by
what we ourselves have done (and not what
others have done for us) that we shall be remem-
bered in after ages.  It requires a constant
watch, and a prayerful heart, on our part to
enable us to keep the right spirit at all times,
for the evil one is always on the alert.  If we
allow ourselves to fall into bad company we will
soon be found drifting in to their ways. There
are many reasons why we should keep good
company, parents may be called away from us
and leave our little brothers and sisters who
must be cared for, how nice it would be to have
an elder brother or sister who would set an ex-
ample that would be worthy of their imitation
and teach them to be orderly and industrious,
in this way they would soon be a great help and
grow up to be self-sustaining.  How much
better this would be than to set examples be-
fore them that would bring them down to shame
and remorse. There are other reasons why we
should keep good company, it is more economi-
cal, if we fall into bad habits we will be apt to
spend more than we make. We should avoid
the fashions and follies of the world, excessive
dress, the use of tea, coffee, tobacco and liquor
are wasteful as well as injurious to the sys-
tem; rather let us use a part of our means in
paying tithing, building temples, etc. We
should not use bad language or slang phrases,
more especially the young ladies, these are very
bad habits and are degrading to the human
mind.  Therefore they should not be indulged
in.  If we will read good books, be humble and
prayerful, we will be built up and strengthened
by so doing, and we will not be very apt to fall
into bad company.  There is an invisible pen
always writing over our heads, making an
exact register of all the transactions of our
lives, not our public conduct only, but the in-
dulgence of our private pleasures, our secret
thoughts and idle hours shall be brought into
account.  Therefore it stands us all in hand to
be noble in word thought and deed. The
Gospel has been revealed for the express pur-
pose of giving us power to control ourselves.
The Lord has told us that if we will seek unto
Him He will aid us to conquer every evil in-
clination and wrong desire.  If we will pursue
this course we will find that our path through
life will be much happier, and we will have
many more friends and will secure the favor of
the Lord.
						E. M. STEVENS.


Hope without action is a broken staff. We
should always hope for things that are possible
and probable.—James Ellis.


HEPHZIBAH.

					III
	She wandered fancy free, o’er hill and dale,
		Or lingered lovingly beside the brook;
	This little wood-nymph of the lowly vale,
		Culling wild flowers from ev’ry sheltered nook. 

	Mrs. H. was a woman of strong character-
istics, tall and dignified in figure, though gentle
and womanly in manner and bearing, her mind
decidedly practical in the full sense of the
term, she proved equal to the emergency that
had overtaken her so unexpectedly. She took
into consideration just what was necessary to
be done to economize and live within her
means, and lost no time in carrying her plans
into execution. Her close attention to the
business of her husband, for the purpose of
helping him, had given her an experience that
but few women in that that day had the opportunity
of acquiring. From the time of her husband's 
death she was so engrossed with cares and re-
sponsibilities that the younger children were
left to themselves, and Hephzibah had ample
opportunity to pursue her idle ways, or only
to visit the old home and wait on grandpa to
hat and cane, and enjoy the freedom of the
orchard, garden, meadows and woods she loved
so well. She was far enough advanced in her
studies to read everything that came in her way
and was passionately fond of books, and stories,
she really believed in fairies and elves and
wondered why she could not be visited by them.
	Her grandpa gave her the most perfect liberty
to rummage over the musty old letters and
manuscript stowed away as useless in the
garret. Tied up in bundles carefully, though
faded soiled and covered in dust, were the writ-
ings and correspondence of her grandmother
for whom she was named, and she would feast
upon these with childish delight, gleaning hero
and there, out of the way and romantic ideas
that accorded with her own peculiar notions.
The neighbors looked on in utter amazement
and wondered what would become of this pre-
cocious child, who was subject to no control,
and who had withal such seemingly exalted
notions, that it totally unfitted her for the com-
panionship of children of her own age. Mrs.
H. would sometimes say to the old Major,
“What shall we do with Hephzibah, she holds
herself so above our country ways?” “Strange
child very, not at all like other children, time
will develop her more fully, she will make her
way in the world;” but he stubbornly refused
to make the sacrifice of her company, or listen
to any plan suggested to induce her to conform
to the usual manner, or condition of little girls.
“I cannot part with her,” he would repeat
over and over.” In spite of her oddity and
her quaint notions she was more sought after
than any of all the village children. She
could repeat more verses and tell more wonder-
ful stories, and knew where all the bird’s nests
were, and when they were ready to fly, and
she never tired of talking in her own way, to
old and young, and with all her lack of con-
formity to other peoples’ ways she was a general
favorite in the village. Even her mother be-
came convinced at last that Hephzibah was of
a different temperament and organization, and
unlike the rest of the family and said to her
older daughters who were solicitious about
the child, “She cannot conform to our habits
of discipline, she must not be forced to submit;


grandpa would say "she is like her grand-
mother's family, she has the Hastings blood
in her veins, we shall see what the future will
unfold." What a puzzle the child was to them
all!
	“What can Hephzibah do with all these
fine aristocratic notions without money," said
her rich uncle to the mother on one occasion
when paying his regular visit to the homestead
he found her installed there as though she
were actually the mistress of ceremonies, posi-
tively  receiving her uncle on his arrival with
the air of a little Duchess bred and born.
He undertook to remonstrate with his father,
telling him how absurd it was to have "that
child assuming so much authority, where she
had no right;" but all to no purpose, the old
man's affection for her was almost an adoration.
So the selfish uncle appealed to the mother, he
knew her to be wise and discriminating and he
carefully inquired what course she intended to
take in regard to Hephzibah's future. "She
cannot go on long in the way she is doing now,"
he said, "for she will soon be developing into
womanhood and you have too many boys and
girls to give her any special advantages; by
and bye you will not be able to control her
at all, if indeed you are now, which appears
doubtful, and the child is far too delicate and
sensitive as well as too proud to push her own
way in the world. You are certainly doing
her a great injustice; she must go away from
here to school, where she will be properly con-
trolled. Once gone from his sight, father will
forget all about it, just like a child, and she
will familiarize herself with other scenes, and
it will change everything for the better." 
	The uncle seemed to think his sister-in-law
coincided with his views, and acquiesced in the
scheme of sending Hephzibah away, but she
did not, far from it—nor did she intend to fol-
low his advice in the least, and when he had
gone she soliloquized thus: "It would never
do to send the child away, while her grandpa
lives, it would break her heart, and the old
gentleman would refuse to be comforted and 
mourn himself to death. She must remain un-
til time brings some change about."
	Friends of the family one after another 
came to offer advice, until Mrs. H. grew weary
of their solicitations, and her replies were such
as to convince them, she knew best herself what
to do with her own, and though some of the
presistent ones felt self-condemned, yet they
tried to make amends for seeming interference,
by praising the child's ability and would say
"we are all interested in her future for we can
see she has more than ordinary talent and it
should be developed in other ways, than hid-
ing herself away in nooks and corners and talk-
to the brooks and birds and trees." 
	Mrs. H. was perfectly well aware of the
situation of affairs, and anxious to have her
little daughter receive an education in the best
schools, and she knew, better than any one
else could, as mothers almost invariably do,
that Hephzibah could not submit to be sent
away from grandpa among strangers, and at
the same time conform to the rigid discipline
of the school-room. She loved her little girl 
with all the warmth of her generous motherly
heart, and her interest in the child grew more
intense still, when she learned how others felt
concerning the child's future, and that she was
such a general favorite in the village. How-
ever she would not yield in the least, and pur-
sued the even tenor of her way, regardless
alike of their admiration of the child, or advice
concerning her welfare. And what of Hephzi-
bah? she was totally unaware of it all, and
still indulged her childish peculiarities; she
never played as other children did, or much
with them at all, though she had likes and dis-
likes, strong ones too, among her playmates as
well as older people; the boys of her own age
were rather fond of annoying and frightening her
[column 2]
when they found her alone in the fields, but
even the mischievous boys held her somehow
in a sort of adoration, and would bring her
the earliest wild-violets and longest-stemmed
water-lilies she loved so well, and thus even the
most bashful ones manifested their boyish
appreciation of her love of the beautiful in 
nature.
	Descended from the staunch old Puritan
stock, with all their severity of character, it
seemed strange that without any predilection
on the part of her parents, she should possess
such enthusiasm on subjects so foreign to the 
habits and customs, as well as the disposition
of the people with whom she associated and
among whom she dwelt. But with all her
weird fancies and rose-colored aspirations, she
had inherited from the Pilgrim fore-fathers and
fore-mothers those traits and attributes, that
must ever lie at the foundation of whatever is
lofty in motive or purpose; honesty, integrity and
a conscientiousness in all her thoughts and
feelings, that gave a moral tone and balance to
the ideal and spiritualistic and kept her within
the bounds of reason even when as she grew
in years, impulse uncontrolled would have led
her into a labyrinth of difficulties.
			[To be continued.]


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
		﻿WESTMINSTER ABBEY.
		BY J. C. KITTREDGE.
		THE CORONATION CHAIRS.
	These are choice and ancient relics. In that
battered chair, the larger of the two, have
been crowned most of England’s sovereigns.
The famous seat was made for Edward I, by
Edward the Painter. It is made of wood, with
four lions carved at the base, and is decidedly
disfigured, giving evidence of hard usage.
Names and figures are rudely cut upon it. It
would seem that insignificant people in earlier
times had the same propensity to seek immor-
tality in that manner as at present. When
used at coronations ornamental covers are
thrown over it. The only time it was ever re-
moved from its present position was when
Cromwell was installed lord protector, in West-
minster hall, thus making the installation of
that great man as prominent and distinguished
as his career. Shakespeare refers to the chair
in Henry VI. It is where Eleanor says to her
husband Humphrey: “Methinks I sate in seat
of majesty, in the cathedral church of West-
minster, and in that chair where kings and
queens are crowned.” Queen Victoria sat in
the same chair at her coronation. The corona-
tions take place in the center of the chancel.
Beneath the chair, is the Stone of Scone, an
object of no less curiosity than the chair itself.
It is not a stone of large dimensions; and was
used in ancient times at the coronation of the
Scottish kings.  The stone has had distin-
guished and various fortunes; there are many
facts and legends connected with its history.
Legend says that the stone was Jacob’s pillow
at Bethel, and carried from thence to Egypt.
Gathelus, the Greek,  came to Egypt and
married Scotia, and it is presumed that he had
something to do with the stone. He subse-
quently fled to Spain. Many years afterward,
as Brek was fishing off Iceland, he pulled it up,
and it was then placed upon the sacred hill of
Taroh. In 330 B.C. Fergus bore the stone to
Dunstaffnage castle, near Oban. Later he
carried it to Iona. In the year 840 A. D.
Kenneth II brought the stone to Scone, near
Perth. All the Scottish kings were crowned
on it, until the time of Baloil. Edward I after
defeating Baloil in 1296, was himself crowned
on it. He had it taken to London, and placed
beneath the coronation chair, as we see it. The
[Column 2]
Scotch were naturally much offended at their
loss of the stone, and later it caused quite a
dispute between Edward III and David II.
The former consented to give up the stone, but
the people of London clamored so loudly about
it that he could return only the regalia. In a
recent book about Scone, published at Perth, is
the following:
	“There is a story told of one of the villagers,
still alive, who went to London on some busi-
ness, which being finished, he resolved on see-
ing some of the sights of the city. Among
other places visited was Westminster Abbey,
where was pointed out to him the stone of
destiny or coronation chair. After standing in
deep meditation for about a minute he accosted
an Englishman standing close by, rather
abruptly with, ‘Ye’ve nae richt tae that stane!’
	“'What do you mean, Sir?’ replied the as-
tonished Englishman.
	“ 'I mean what I say, an’ that’s ye’ve nae
richt tae that stane, because it wis stolen frae
my native toon,' thundered forth our friend.
	“‘My dear Sir, I cannot comprehend,' re-
plied the still more astonished Englishman.
	“‘Weel, Sir, if ye dinna ken, I’ll tell ye.
Ane o’ your thievish kings, Edward, took that
stane awa frae Scoon, an’ ye’ve never ha’en as
muckle honesty about ye as tae return it.’ ”
	He turned on his heel, and walked off, leav-
ing his friend to solve the problem as best he
could.
	It is interesting to trace the various migra-
tions of the famous stone, not to mention the
apocryphal part of the story of its coming from
Dunstaffnage castle, near Oban (a fine ruin
which we have seen and elsewhere described),
to the time when the Scots were defeated by
the Picts, who took it to Scone, near Perth.
There it remained until removed to West-
minster abbey by Edward III. Thus we see
the famous stone to have been an epitome of
the momentous events which moved the affairs
of history; fittingly expressing the conquest
and unity of Scotland with England. The
other chair, which is of far later date, is occu-
pied by the consorts. It was made at the time
of Mary II.
	On the platform behind the chairs, are the
shield and sword of Edward III, carried be-
fore him in France; the latter is seven feet in
length. Other monuments within the chapel,
not previously referred to, are those of Almer
de Valence, Earl Pembroke, Aveline, Countess
of Lancaster, Edmund Crouchback, Earl of
Lancaster, son of Henry III.
		SHRINE OF SAINT ERASMUS.
	This is perhaps the most picturesque section
of the abbey. It dates from the time of
Richard II.

NOTES AND NEWS.

	Florence Nightingale has written a letter to
the Johnstown sufferers.
	“Universal Suffrage,” said Charles Sumer,
“is the first proof and only basis of a genuine
republic.”
	The woman suffragists have scored a victory
in the Washington Convention in the election
of Judge Hoyt president, and Dr. John R.
Thompson, chaplain.
	The daughter of the Chief Justice of the
United States is to enter a law office and study
for the legal profession. She has just gradu-
ated from college.
	Mrs. Oliphant, the novelist, is sixty-three
years old and a hard worker, with no desire for
newspaper fame. She lives with her two
children near Windsor castle.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


THE FIRST GRANDCHILD.

"Grandmother,” called the father, and there came
Out from the vine wreathed porch a blushing dame,
Surprised and eager at the strange new name.

The clock within rang forth the chime for eight,
“A message? Read it—quick—how can you wait?"
Her husband, smiling, leaned upon the gate.

At arm’s length holding in his trembling hand
The crisp, white sheet, while he the writing scanned,
Then read once more with voice almost unmanned.

" 'Thy grand-daughter salutes thee,’ ‘Baby Bell,’
"Mother and child, thank God, are doing well.''
A moment’s silence on the proud twain fell.

She broke it soon, "Grandfather, I congrat—”
''What, me?’ the good man cried, lifting his hat,—
"‘Grandfather’—me? I hadn’t thought of that.''
						—Harper's Weekly,


[For the EXPONENT.]
“SHALL NEVER  COME MESSAGE OR
				TOKEN."

	Shall never come message or token,
		Ne'er a sound to break silence dread—
	Nor one tender word breathed or spoken,
		Assuring that love is not dead?

	Towards the far away snow-capped mountains
		And over its shadowly vale,
	Goes forth the sad cry, but the echo
		Weird, mournful, returns on the gale.

	I wait in the gloaming of eve’ing,
		Her bright banners fade into hlue.
	I question, can love that was golden
		Fade and die from heart leal and true?

	Ah! somewhere the sun must be shining,
		Somewhere, tho’ now hidden from sight,
	And tho' we see not through the darkness,
		We know that beyond there is light.

	In hope, we'll wait on thro' the gloaming,
		Tho' no voice replies as of yore;
	We know there must come a morning
		Where love reigns supreme evermore,
							L. M. HEWLINGS.


OUR MOTHER.

DEDICATED TO THE HUSBAND AND FAMILY OF MRS.
					ELIZA FOX.

Our mother has gone to her long peaceful rest, 
Angels have borne her to the home of the blest;
Loved ones will receive her where friendships are
	rife,
Our beloved one has passed from death unto life.

Then why should we mourn her? Let God's will be
	done!
The Lord has released her, the battle is won;
Oh, would we recall her earth’s pains to endure
Our mother so noble, unselfish and pure?

Recall her? O no! Her afflictions are o'er;
Our Fatber, He knows what the future’s in store;
Perhaps He has called her some trial to spare,
Mothers’s earth-work is finished, she has suffered her
	share.

God grant we may live in her footprints to tread,
That when we've completed our work ’twill be said
As we say of her, "thou hast done thy work well,
Thy glory and honor no mortal can tell.''

It seems in mind’s vision, we almost can see
Thy spirit so bouyant, so happy and free;
Thy hand in thy guide’s as he softly says “come,'' 
And opens the gates to thy beautiful home.

Our minds reach out farther, we see thee within
And Frankie, thy daughter, impatient to win
From thy lips, the news thou hast brought of her boy
The vision is closed; we leave thee with thy joy.
								R. M. Fox.


[Column 1]
PLEASANT GROVE.
	Special meeting at the meeting house May
20, 1889, President E. Brown presiding. After
singing, prayer was offered by Elder Jno. Todd.
Singing, "Now let us rejoice." President
Brown announced the presence of the visiting
sisters, viz: President Zina D. Young, also
President Mary John and Counselor Marilla
Daniels, of Utah Stake. The secretary then
read the minutes of the previous meeting.
	President John:—“There are many subjects
we could speak upon. We live in an important
time, and it requires us to be prayerful and
diligent continually, that we may escape the
great calamities that will come upon the in-
habitants of the earth. The ladies' journal,
the EXPONENT, is not supported by us as it
should be. This is a valuable paper and the
price has been reduced to $1.00 per year.
	Counselor M. Daniels:—“This is the first
opportunity I have had of meeting with the
sisters of this place. The first hymn sung es-
pecially, is full of inspiration; these old hymns
bring up happy reminiscenses of the early scenes
in this Church. Our persecutions to-day are
of a different nature to those endured by the
Saints in the rise of this Church. Let us meet
everything bravely, striving to perform our
duty with diligence, then it makes no difference
what our trials are, whether we live or die, it
will be well with us. Many of our sisters are
passing under the rod of affliction; let us help
them and extend to them the charity which is
the true love of God. Our mission is to cheer
and comfort each other, read good books and
inform our minds that we may act intelligently,
encourage the young to read the Church works
and become informed in the history of our
people.
	President Z. D. Young:—Very few faces do
I recognize—but we are all kindred spirits.
Paul says: "When I was a child I spake as a
child, I understood as a child, I thought as a
child; but when I became a man, I put away
childish things." We must become perfect
even as our Father in heaven is perfect.
Brother Joseph said: "The fulness of the
gospel includes the organization of the sisters."
You should meet together as often as twice a
month, as we are too apt to grow cold if we
only have monthly meetings. Sisters when 
you are necessitated to leave an office ask to be
honorably released. I do not like the word
"resign;" this is a retrograde word.
	The spirits of all those persons who held the
priesthood in any dispensation, are prepared
for the mission in the spirit world, then how
necessary is the proper care of our bodies, that
the progress of the spirit may not be retarded
by being placed in a weakly tabernacle. Our
mode of dress has much to do with our health,
we want to learn how to breathe, did you ever
think of that? Notice the difference in breath-
ing when we undress to retire; tight lacing is
very injurious.
	Sister Zina said many of our brethren say
that the trial of imprisonment is not so great
as what their families endure in their absence.
The finest flour is ground the hardest. If we
can acknowledge the hand of God in all
things, then He is our support. Let us exer-
cise faith to endure persecution. The Lord
will cause the wrath of man to praise Him.
The scene at Sister Taylor's grave in the ab-
sence of her husband was a sad one, though I
do not like to live in the past only as it reflects
sunshine upon the future. Sisters do encourage
the young girls to attend their meetings; 
do not puff the children up in pride, but rather
write proper thoughts upon the tablets of their
souls. Girls, do not be in too big a hurry to
leave the primary. The young ladies' associ-
ations are very important as its members are
the key to our coming nation. A certain chief

[Column 2]
caused the young men of his tribe to be highly 
educated, but the next generation had no
semblance of the education of their fathers.
The chief said he had made a grand mistake,
the mother should be educated as well, as they
are the ones who teach the children.
	President Young said many years ago
"there is silk in these elements." If we had
obeyed his counsel in regard to it we could 
have produced half a million in silk. By in-
dustry the children should learn to know the
value of a dollar. Sisters, study human na-
ture and gain the affections of your sons and
daughters, let young people be young, assist in
making amusement for them—half of our
troubles in controlling our children are caused
through ignorance. When I meet so many of
our aged sisters who have lived pure lives my
heart swells with gratitude, and thouh I
never was a Methodist, I feel like shouting
hosannah to God. I am proud of the Saints. 
	Senator Wise, in visiting the large taber-
nacle and viewing the grand organ, lifted his
hat and said, "Surely this is not the work of a
degraded people." While visiting in Provo
with an Eastern lady we called at the B. Y.
Academy, and she was surprised to see such 
advantages here in education and remarked 
upon the fine appearance of the young men.
Strangers feel the presence of the purity and
virtue shed forth among this people, now if we
live up to the Gospel truths it will bring us to
perfection. Teach our children if they are
sick to exercise faith to be healed, nurse with
mild herbs, and that not by the hand of an
enemy. If we hold our minds in proper con-
dition the truths we hear will be brought to our 
remembrance. Sisters, do not get discouraged,
President Young has said that to the real saint
there would be no greater trials than we have
already had. Let us cease speaking evil of
one another, and from all lightmindedness,
loud laughter, etc. We find the most beautiful
rules on etiquette laid down in the Doctrine
and Covenants.
	Bishop Brown said he had been highly
entertained in listening to the wise instructions
of the sisters, they, like the Elders, feel a
weakness in addressing the Saints. This is
right and proper, we should lean upon the
Lord for His Spirit to guide us. God says the
mission of woman is to bear the souls of men.
What a grand mission to bear! Teaching and
implanting in their minds the seeds of eternal
life, the influence of the mother stays with the
child through life; if these impressions are
wrong, that is likely to be the character of the
man or woman. In my mission, too, I found
the most devout Elders with unbelieving wives
unable to bring their children into the Church,
while if the mother is the believer and the
father not, the opposite is the restlt: The most
important duty of this organization is to look
after the poor and afflicted, also to elevate and
prepare the rising generation to bear off the
kingdom. The honest and upright of the
world will come to Zion to behold her beauty
and intelligence.
	President E. Brown made closing remarks:
Let us endeavor to live by the light we receive
from time to time.
	Singing—The Spirit of God like a fire is
burning. Benediction by Bishop Brown,


Socrates called beauty a short-lived tyranny;
Plato, a privilege of nature; Theophrastus, a
silent cheat; Theocritus, a delightful prejudice;
Carneades, a solitary kingdom; Domitian said
that nothing was more grateful; Aristotle af-
firmed that beauty was better than all. the let-
ters of recommendation in the world; Homer,
that ‘twas a glorious gift of nature; and Ovid,
alluding to him, calls it a favor bestowed, by
the gods.—From the Italian.
